payload.professional_persona: Thaneswar Sing, a 35‑year‑old tea garden operative with hands‑on expertise in leaf plucking, irrigation management and basic carpentry, applies a methodical and curiosity‑driven approach to streamline productivity, and they reliably coordinate small teams while planning work schedules with the discipline of a seasoned supervisor.
payload.linguistic_persona: Thaneswar Sing, a 35‑year‑old who grew up speaking the local language of their tea garden village fluently, has functional reading and writing skills in Hindi and can decipher basic English signage, though they rely primarily on their native tongue for daily communication.
payload.sports_persona: Thaneswar Sing, a 35‑year‑old tea garden resident who avidly follows cricket on television, cheering for the Chennai Super Kings in the IPL and occasionally participates in informal football matches organized by the tea garden sports club during weekends, and they enjoy the camaraderie of the fellow players.
payload.arts_persona: Thaneswar Sing, a 35‑year‑old cultural enthusiast who enjoys listening to Bihu rhythms, playing the dhol during festivals, devouring poetry by Bhupen Hazarika and Zubeen Garg’s soulful songs, and they unwind with Assamese cinema classics such as "Village Rockstars".
payload.travel_persona: Thaneswar Sing, a 35‑year‑old who prefers modest road trips to nearby hill stations like Shillong and wildlife safaris in Kaziranga, plans vacations around school holidays, and they balance wanderlust with the practicalities of supporting a growing family on a tea garden income.
payload.culinary_persona: Thaneswar Sing, a 35‑year‑old who relishes traditional dishes such as masor tenga, duck khar, and steaming pitha made with rice flour and jaggery, enjoys brewing strong tea from garden leaves, and can competently cook these home‑style meals though they rarely experiment beyond familiar flavors.
payload.persona: Thaneswar Sing, a diligent tea‑garden supervisor‑in‑the‑making who tunes Bihu drums in solitude, saves every rupee in a ledger, yet occasionally splurges on cricket channel fees, and they embody a mix of methodical thrift and quiet passion.
payload.cultural_background: Thaneswar Sing belongs to a rural Assamese community in Dibrugarh, a region known for its tea gardens and rich cultural heritage. His family follows Assamese customs, celebrates Bihu festivals with dance and music, and maintains close ties with neighbours in the village. The agrarian lifestyle, community solidarity and respect for nature shape his worldview.
payload.linguistic_background: His mother tongue is Assamese; Thaneswar reads, writes and speaks it fluently. He completed middle school, which provided basic literacy in Assamese and a rudimentary understanding of Hindi and English. He can comprehend simple Hindi instructions and read basic English signage related to his work, but he is not proficient in either language. His primary communication is in Assamese, especially at home and within the tea garden community.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Thaneswar possesses practical expertise in tea garden operations, including tea leaf plucking, garden maintenance, irrigation management, and basic carpentry for constructing garden structures. He is adept at record keeping of daily yields, coordinating small teams of laborers, and applying systematic approaches to improve productivity. His organized nature also makes him skilled in planning work schedules and managing simple finances for household needs.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Tea leaf plucking', 'Garden maintenance', 'Irrigation management', 'Basic carpentry', 'Yield record keeping', 'Team coordination', 'Work schedule planning', 'Simple household finance management']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Thaneswar enjoys listening to Bihu music, playing the dhol during festive occasions, reading Assamese literature and poetry, cooking traditional Assamese dishes, tending his small kitchen garden, watching cricket matches on television, and taking leisurely walks through the tea gardens to unwind.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Bihu music', 'Playing the dhol', 'Reading Assamese literature', 'Cooking Assamese dishes', 'Gardening', 'Watching cricket', 'Walking in tea gardens']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: He aims to become a supervisor in the tea garden, improve his leadership skills, learn modern tea processing techniques, and eventually start his own small tea stall or diversify his family's income through additional agricultural activities. He also aspires to acquire basic financial literacy to better manage household finances and ensure a stable future for his children.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 35-year-old male from rural Dibrugarh, Assam, currently married, with a middle school education. He works as a tapper in the tea industry and speaks Assamese as his first language. Clinically, he exhibits methodical thrift, saving money in a ledger, while occasionally spending on cricket channel fees. His lifestyle includes a sedentary job with limited physical activity and a diet rich in traditional Assamese dishes.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Prescription for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Assamese (code=as, script=Bengali). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Assam, district=Dibrugarh.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Bengali for Assamese.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 35
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Tappers, Other
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Rural
- state: Assam
- district: Dibrugarh
- first_language: Assamese
- clinical_persona_summary: A 35-year-old male from rural Dibrugarh, Assam, currently married, with a middle school education. He works as a tapper in the tea industry and speaks Assamese as his first language. Clinically, he exhibits methodical thrift, saving money in a ledger, while occasionally spending on cricket channel fees. His lifestyle includes a sedentary job with limited physical activity and a diet rich in traditional Assamese dishes.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Dibrugarh Civil Hospital]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Ranjan Gogoi]], [[AGE|35]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-2024-0815-001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Pranab Kalita]]
Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
ABHA ID: [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
Patient ID: [[PATIENT_ID|PID-DG-2024-0815]]
Address: [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|Village: Borpathar, P.O. Borpathar, District: Dibrugarh, Assam - 786001]]
District: [[DISTRICT|Dibrugarh]]

Chief Complaint:
The patient presents with a painful, swollen right hand following a workplace injury sustained three days ago while operating a tea leaf tapper machine. He reports increasing pain and difficulty in moving his fingers.

Examination:
On examination, there is a 3x2 cm laceration over the dorsum of the right hand with underlying tendon exposure. The wound is contaminated with soil particles. There is marked swelling and tenderness over the thenar eminence. Capillary refill is intact. No signs of infection are noted at the site.

Diagnosis:
Open flexor tendon injury of the right hand with secondary contamination.

Plan:
1. Urgent surgical exploration and primary repair of the flexor tendon under regional anesthesia.
2. Thorough wound debridement and irrigation.
3. Primary closure of the skin after repair.
4. Post-operative antibiotics and tetanus prophylaxis.
5. Hand therapy referral for rehabilitation.

Medications:
- [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
- Amoxicillin 500 mg, one tablet three times a day for 5 days.
- Diclofenac 50 mg, one tablet twice a day as needed for pain.
- Tablet Paracetamol 650 mg, one tablet four times a day as needed for pain.

Follow-up:
Patient advised to return for post-operative review in 7 days.

Prescribed by:
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Pranab Kalita]]

payload.travel_persona: Satyendra Kumar prefers well‑planned short trips, having explored the tea gardens of Darjeeling and the historic lanes of Kolkata, and they dream of a pilgrimage to the Taj Mahal and a weekend getaway to the serene hills of Sikkim, balancing travel aspirations with limited family finances.
payload.culinary_persona: Satyendra Kumar prefers Bengali cuisine, delighting in fish curry (macher jhol), aloo posto (potato with poppy seed paste), and mishti doi, and while they can whip up a simple dish at home, they often order meals from the local tiffin service to save time.
payload.persona: Satyendra Kumar blends disciplined rural pragmatism with vibrant Bengali cultural flair, preferring early‑morning field walks, structured savings, and quiet evenings spent listening to Rabindra Sangeet, though they occasionally indulge in impulsive sweet purchases.
payload.cultural_background: Satyendra Kumar comes from a Bengali Hindu family that has long been rooted in the rural outskirts of Katihar, Bihar. payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]

  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]

  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Hospital Billing / Invoice for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Bengali (code=bn, script=Bengali). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Bihar, district=Katihar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Bengali for Bengali.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once (parking/ambulance or motor claim line). Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 25
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Salesman, Retail Trade
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Rural
- state: Bihar
- district: Katihar
- first_language: Bengali
- clinical_persona_summary: Satyendra Kumar is a 25-year-old male from rural Katihar, Bihar, who is never married and works as a salesman in retail trade. He has a secondary education and speaks Bengali as his first language, with conversational Hindi proficiency. His lifestyle includes early-morning walks, structured savings, and listening to Rabindra Sangeet.

Document format guide for Hospital Billing / Invoice (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Invoice — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] | Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | MRN [[MRN|...]]
Address district [[DISTRICT|...]] PIN [[PIN_CODE|110001]]
Ambulance / parking vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
Line items with amounts (no entity tags on procedure names).

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PIN_CODE — ZIP / Postal Code (PIN Code) e.g. [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- AADHAAR_NUMBER — Aadhaar Number e.g. [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
- INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER — Insurance Policy Number e.g. [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
- TAX_ID — Tax Identification Number (TIN/GSTIN) e.g. [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
- TELEPHONE_LANDLINE — Telephone Number (Landline) e.g. [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.

- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Discharge Summary for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Bengali (code=bn, script=Bengali). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Bihar, district=Katihar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Bengali for Bengali.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 25
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Salesman, Retail Trade
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Rural
- state: Bihar
- district: Katihar
- first_language: Bengali
- clinical_persona_summary: A 25-year-old male from rural Katihar, Bihar, who is never married, works as a salesman in retail trade, and has a secondary education. He speaks Bengali at home and is conversational in Hindi. His lifestyle includes early-morning walks, structured savings, and listening to Rabindra Sangeet.

Document format guide for Discharge Summary (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] DOB [[DOB|1994-06-21]] [[AGE|30]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]] | Course / advice: <concise paragraphs matching domain + persona sex/age>

MANDATORY inline entities (14 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- ABHA_ADDRESS — ABHA Address e.g. [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
- CASTE — Caste e.g. [[CASTE|OBC]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Katihar District Hospital]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ranjan Kumar]] DOB [[DOB|2000-01-15]] [[AGE|25]] [[GENDER|Male]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-001]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|A1]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|05]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Amit Kumar]] | Course / advice: Patient was admitted with complaints of high-grade fever, cough, and breathlessness for 5 days. On examination, he was febrile with tachycardia and bilateral crepitations in the chest. Investigations revealed leukocytosis and elevated C-reactive protein. A chest X-ray showed infiltrates in the right lower lobe. He was diagnosed with community-acquired pneumonia and managed with IV antibiotics, antipyretics, and supportive care. His condition improved significantly over the next 4 days, with resolution of fever and improvement in respiratory symptoms. He is now afebrile, clinically stable, and ready for discharge. He has been advised to complete a 5-day course of oral antibiotics at home, continue with steam inhalation, and follow up with his primary care physician in one week. He should also get his annual flu vaccine.

[[RELATIVE_NAME|Sanjay Kumar]]
[[DISTRICT|Katihar]]
[[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
[[ABHA_ADDRESS|ranjan.kumar@abdm]]
[[CASTE|SC]]

payload.professional_persona: An ambitious retail professional, Satyendra Kumar combines practical sales expertise and meticulous inventory tracking, and they aim to become a store supervisor within three years while planning to eventually open a modest grocery outlet for the local community.
payload.linguistic_persona: Fluent in Bengali, Satyendra Kumar comfortably converses in Hindi for everyday interactions and manages basic written tasks in the language, while they have functional English proficiency that allows them to read instructions and signage but is rarely used in daily conversation.
payload.sports_persona: Satyendra Kumar follows cricket passionately, cheering for the Indian national team and the Kolkata Knight Riders on weekends, and they occasionally join friends for informal street cricket matches in the village ground.
payload.arts_persona: Satyendra Kumar immerses in the arts by listening to Rabindra Sangeet on his phone, reading Bengali short stories by Satyajit Ray, and they enjoy classic Bollywood films starring Amitabh Bachchan, which provide a cultural anchor amidst his rural routine.
payload.travel_persona: Satyendra Kumar prefers well‑planned short trips, having explored the tea gardens of Darjeeling and the historic lanes of Kolkata, and they dream of a pilgrimage to the Taj Mahal and a weekend getaway to the serene hills of Sikkim, balancing travel aspirations with limited family finances.
payload.culinary_persona: Satyendra Kumar prefers Bengali cuisine, delighting in fish curry (macher jhol), aloo posto (potato with poppy seed paste), and mishti doi, and while they can whip up a simple dish at home, they often order meals from the local tiffin service to save time.
payload.persona: Satyendra Kumar blends disciplined rural pragmatism with vibrant Bengali cultural flair, preferring early‑morning field walks, structured savings, and quiet evenings spent listening to Rabindra Sangeet, though they occasionally indulge in impulsive sweet purchases.
payload.cultural_background: Satyendra Kumar comes from a Bengali Hindu family that has long been rooted in the rural outskirts of Katihar, Bihar. While his household preserves Bengali traditions—such as speaking Bengali at home, celebrating Durga Puja with elaborate pandals, and preparing fish curry and sweets—he also lives amidst Bihari customs like Chhath Puja, Bhojpuri folk songs, and the simple, community‑centered rhythm of agrarian life. This blend of Bengali cultural vibrancy and the pragmatic, close‑knit ethos of rural Bihar shapes his worldview.
payload.linguistic_background: His mother tongue is Bengali; he reads and writes it fluently, having completed his secondary education in a Bengali‑medium school. Growing up in a Hindi‑dominant region, he is comfortably conversational in Hindi, understands spoken Hindi well, and can manage everyday written tasks such as filling simple forms or reading local notices, although his formal Hindi writing skills are limited to practical usage. His matric‑level schooling gave him a functional grasp of English, enough to read basic instructions, signs, and occasional pamphlets, but he rarely uses English in his daily interactions.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Satyendra has built practical sales and retail capabilities suited to a small town environment. He greets customers warmly, quickly assesses their immediate needs, and suggests suitable products, fostering repeat business. He handles cash transactions accurately, maintains simple inventory ledgers, and operates a mobile point‑of‑sale (POS) device for billing. His negotiation skills enable him to obtain favorable rates from local wholesalers, and he applies basic accounting principles to track daily sales and manage petty cash. He stays attuned to local market trends, especially seasonal produce and festival‑related goods, allowing him to stock high‑demand items.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Customer service', 'Needs assessment', 'Cash handling', 'Inventory management', 'Mobile POS operation', 'Basic accounting', 'Negotiation with wholesalers', 'Local market trend awareness', 'Product recommendation', 'Record keeping']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Satyendra enjoys listening to Rabindra Sangeet on his mobile phone, reading Bengali short stories, and watching cricket matches on the local TV channel. He maintains a small vegetable patch behind his house and likes taking early‑morning walks in the fields surrounding his village. During festivals, he participates in community events in a modest role, helping set up decorations or distribute sweets. Cooking traditional Bengali dishes such as fish curry and mishti doi also brings him joy.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Rabindra Sangeet', 'Reading Bengali short stories', 'Watching cricket on TV', 'Gardening vegetable patch', 'Morning walks in fields', 'Participating in community festivals (small role)', 'Cooking traditional Bengali dishes']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Satyendra aims to climb the retail ladder by becoming a store supervisor within the next three years, overseeing a larger shop and a small team. In the longer term, he dreams of opening his own modest grocery outlet in Katihar, offering quality products at fair prices to serve his community. To reach this, he plans to save a portion of his monthly income, attend short‑term business training programs run by local NGOs, and improve his Hindi writing and basic accounting skills. He also hopes to achieve financial stability to support his parents and to fund his younger sister’s education.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Satyendra Kumar is a 25-year-old male from rural Katihar, Bihar, who works as a salesman in retail trade. He is never married, has completed matriculation, and speaks Bengali as his first language while being conversational in Hindi. His lifestyle includes early-morning walks, structured savings, and listening to Rabindra Sangeet.

payload.generated_text:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-0815]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Katihar Sadar Hospital]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajiv Kumar]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Satyendra Kumar]], [[AGE|25]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|Katihar]]
Reason: Persistent cough with constitutional symptoms for evaluation and management of suspected pulmonary tuberculosis and assessment of co-morbid non-communicable diseases.

Dear Colleague,

We are referring [[PATIENT_NAME|Satyendra Kumar]], a 25-year-old male resident of [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]], [[DISTRICT|Katihar]], for further evaluation and management. The patient presents with a three-week history of progressive cough, initially dry and now productive of scant white sputum, associated with low-grade evening fevers, night sweats, and a 5 kg weight loss over the past month. He reports mild shortness of breath on exertion.

On examination, the patient is conscious and oriented, with a temperature of 37.8°C, heart rate of 98 bpm, respiratory rate of 18 breaths/min, and blood pressure of 120/80 mmHg. Chest auscultation reveals coarse crackles in the right upper zone. No clubbing or lymphadenopathy is noted.

Given the clinical picture, a provisional diagnosis of pulmonary tuberculosis is suspected. Investigations including sputum for GeneXpert MTB/RIF, chest X-ray, and complete blood count have been ordered at our facility. The patient's [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] is attached for your reference.

The patient's father, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Kumar]], will accompany him and can be reached at [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]. For identification purposes, the patient's [[DRIVING_LICENCE|BR-19-2024-0012345]] and [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|A1234567]] are available.

We request you to kindly evaluate the patient, confirm the diagnosis, and initiate the standard anti-tubercular therapy as per the national program. Given his age and occupation, a baseline assessment for any non-communicable disease risk factors would also be beneficial.

Thank you for your attention to this case.

Sincerely,
[[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajiv Kumar]]
[[HOSPITAL_NAME|Katihar Sadar Hospital]]
[[PHONE_NUMBER|0640-2545678]]

payload.professional_persona: An aspiring senior salt reclamation technician, Kenshing Paul combines hands‑on expertise in brine management and pump maintenance with a vivid imagination for low‑cost solar‑assisted drying, and they meticulously plan each shift with detailed checklists.
payload.linguistic_persona: Linguistically adept, Kenshing Paul speaks their mother tongue fluently and writes it in Devanagari, reads and writes Hindi at a functional level, converses comfortably in Assamese for marketplace interactions, and picks up basic English phrases from church sermons, allowing them to switch languages as the situation demands.
payload.sports_persona: A keen cricket enthusiast, Kenshing Paul plays weekly street matches with friends, avidly follows the Assam cricket team and the Delhi Capitals in the IPL, and they keep fit by jogging through the salt fields during early mornings.
payload.arts_persona: A creative soul, Kenshing Paul spends afternoons sketching the salt ponds and surrounding fields, drawing inspiration from regional folk singer Lunjor Mingi’s melodies and Bollywood vocalist Arijit Singh’s emotive ballads, while sharpening techniques through tutorials on the YouTube channel ‘Art of Sketching’.
payload.travel_persona: A modest traveler, Kenshing Paul enjoys weekend trips to nearby attractions like Kaziranga National Park and Shillong, dreams of a pilgrimage to Sikkim's Buddhist monasteries, carefully plans each journey to stay within a tight budget, and they prefer traveling with family during festivals but treasure solitary walks in the fields for inspiration.
payload.culinary_persona: A food enthusiast, Kenshing Paul relishes Assamese staple maasor tenga, regional pitha and bamboo‑shoot curries seasoned with mustard oil, often savors a cup of chai with jaggery after sketching, and while they can whip up simple dal‑roti at home, they prefer exploring street stalls for fresh fish fry and mango‑based desserts.
payload.persona: Kenshing Paul is a meticulous 18‑year‑old salt‑pond technician who blends artistic imagination with a lingering worry about the future, often juggling sketchbooks, cricket bat, and a nervous habit of checking equipment twice.
payload.cultural_background: Kenshing Paul belongs to the Bodo community, an indigenous tribal group of Assam, and lives in the rural village of Chirang. As a Christian, his family’s faith blends with Bodo customs, celebrating both church festivals and traditional Bodo rites. Growing up in a salt‑rich area, he is familiar with the region’s agricultural and salt reclamation practices, while also being exposed to Assamese and Hindi cultural elements through schooling and community interactions.
payload.linguistic_background: Kenshing grew up speaking Bodo at home and is fluent in speaking, reading, and writing Bodo using the Devanagari script. His schooling in a government school taught him Hindi, where he achieved functional literacy, enabling him to read newspapers, official documents, and simple literature. He also learned Assamese as a third language, primarily for oral communication with neighbors and market interactions; his reading and writing skills in Assamese are rudimentary. Additionally, he has basic exposure to English through church services and occasional media.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Kenshing is proficient in salt reclamation processes, including brine management, evaporation, and crystallization techniques. He operates and maintains simple mechanical equipment such as pumps, filters, and drying racks used in the salt ponds. His middle‑school education equips him with basic arithmetic and record‑keeping abilities for tracking production volumes and labor hours. He practices safety protocols, conducts routine equipment checks, and provides basic first‑aid assistance on site. Additionally, his imaginative side fosters a talent for sketching and visualising process improvements, and his organized nature makes him reliable in planning daily work schedules and coordinating with fellow workers.

payload.hobbies_and_interests: Kenshing enjoys sketching rural landscapes and salt pond scenes, reading Hindi novels and Christian literature, listening to traditional Bodo folk songs, playing cricket with friends, and experimenting with simple DIY projects at home. He also likes taking solitary walks in the fields, gardening, and learning new art techniques through online videos whenever internet access permits.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Sketching rural landscapes", "Reading Hindi novels and Christian literature", "Listening to Bodo folk music", "Playing cricket", "DIY home projects", "Solitary walks in fields", "Gardening", "Learning new art techniques online"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Kenshing aspires to become a senior technician or supervisor in the salt reclamation sector, mastering advanced evaporation and crystallisation methods. He plans to enroll in a vocational training program to acquire certifications in mechanical maintenance and environmental safety. In the long term, he wishes to introduce low‑cost, solar‑assisted salt drying techniques that improve productivity for his village’s cooperative. He also aims to secure a stable income that supports his young family, while continuing to nurture his creative side by documenting process innovations through sketches and simple reports.

payload.professional_persona: An aspiring senior salt reclamation technician, Kenshing Paul combines hands‑on expertise in brine management and pump maintenance with a vivid imagination for low‑cost solar‑assisted drying, and they meticulously plan each shift with detailed checklists.
payload.linguistic_persona: Linguistically adept, Kenshing Paul speaks their mother tongue fluently and writes it in Devanagari, reads and writes Hindi at a functional level, converses comfortably in Assamese for marketplace interactions, and picks up basic English phrases from church sermons, allowing them to switch languages as the situation demands.
payload.sports_persona: A keen cricket enthusiast, Kenshing Paul plays weekly street matches with friends, avidly follows the Assam cricket team and the Delhi Capitals in the IPL, and they keep fit by jogging through the salt fields during early mornings.
payload.arts_persona: A creative soul, Kenshing Paul spends afternoons sketching the salt ponds and surrounding fields, drawing inspiration from regional folk singer Lunjor Mingi’s melodies and Bollywood vocalist Arijit Singh’s emotive ballads, while sharpening techniques through tutorials on the YouTube channel ‘Art of Sketching’.
payload.travel_persona: A modest traveler, Kenshing Paul enjoys weekend trips to nearby attractions like Kaziranga National Park and Shillong, dreams of a pilgrimage to Sikkim's Buddhist monasteries, carefully plans each journey to stay within a tight budget, and they prefer traveling with family during festivals but treasure solitary walks in the fields for inspiration.
payload.culinary_persona: A food enthusiast, Kenshing Paul relishes Assamese staple maasor tenga, regional pitha and bamboo‑shoot curries seasoned with mustard oil, often savors a cup of chai with jaggery after sketching, and while they can whip up simple dal‑roti at home, they prefer exploring street stalls for fresh fish fry and mango‑based desserts.
payload.persona: Kenshing Paul is a meticulous 18‑year‑old salt‑pond technician who blends artistic imagination with a lingering worry about the future, often juggling sketchbooks, cricket bat, and a nervous habit of checking equipment twice.
payload.cultural_background: Kenshing Paul belongs to the Bodo community, an indigenous tribal group of Assam, and lives in the rural village of Chirang. As a Christian, his family’s faith blends with Bodo customs, celebrating both church festivals and traditional Bodo rites. Growing up in a salt‑rich area, he is familiar with the region’s agricultural and salt reclamation practices, while also being exposed to Assamese and Hindi cultural elements through schooling and community interactions.
payload.linguistic_background: Kenshing grew up speaking Bodo at home and is fluent in speaking, reading, and writing Bodo using the Devanagari script. His schooling in a government school taught him Hindi, where he achieved functional literacy, enabling him to read newspapers, official documents, and simple literature. He also learned Assamese as a third language, primarily for oral communication with neighbors and market interactions; his reading and writing skills in Assamese are rudimentary. Additionally, he has basic exposure to English through church services and occasional media.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Kenshing is proficient in salt reclamation processes, including brine management, evaporation, and crystallization techniques. payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]

  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]

  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]

  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]

  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]

  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]

  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete PHC Register Entry (Primary Health Centre) for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Bodo (code=brx, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Assam, district=Chirang.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Bodo.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 18
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Salt Reclamation Worker
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Rural
- state: Assam
- district: Chirang
- first_language: Bodo
- clinical_persona_summary: A 18‑year‑old male salt reclamation worker from rural Chirang, Assam, who is married and has completed middle education. He is fluent in Bodo and functional Hindi, and works as a salt‑pond technician. Clinically, he presents with a history of repetitive equipment checks and a habit of sketching during downtime. He is a keen cricket player and follows the Assam cricket team.

Document format guide for PHC Register Entry (Primary Health Centre) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
PHC register — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC ...]] Village [[VILLAGE|...]] District [[DISTRICT|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|19]] [[GENDER|Female]] | brief entry line

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
PHC register — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Bhangbari]] Village [[VILLAGE|Bhangbari]] District [[DISTRICT|Chirang]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Ranjit Boro]] [[AGE|18]] [[GENDER|Male]] | Cough for 3 weeks, weight loss, evening fever

Patient is an 18-year-old male salt reclamation worker from Bhangbari village, Chirang district. He presents with a productive cough for three weeks, associated with evening low-grade fever and a 5 kg weight loss over the past month. He reports night sweats and decreased appetite. On examination, he is conscious, oriented, and afebrile. Respiratory examination reveals reduced breath sounds in the right upper zone with occasional rhonchi. His vitals are stable. A provisional diagnosis of pulmonary tuberculosis is made. Investigations ordered include sputum for GeneXpert MTB/RIF and a chest X-ray. He has been started on the standard anti-tubercular therapy regimen under DOTS. The [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Basumatary]] will be providing directly observed treatment. Patient's [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-AS-2023-0045]] and [[VOTER_ID|BR-1234567]] details are verified. His [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]] is linked to his health record. Contact number [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] is confirmed. Follow-up scheduled in one week.

payload.cultural_background: Ritu hails from the Dogri‑speaking community of Reasi in Jammu & Kashmir. --- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Shanti Devi]] [[AGE|20]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9419123456]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|shanti.devi.reasi@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sub-District Hospital Reasi]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Namaste doctor saheb, I am [[PATIENT_NAME|Shanti Devi]] from Reasi. I have been having a persistent headache for the past three days. It's a dull ache, mostly on the right side of my head, and it gets worse when I bend down. I also feel a bit nauseous sometimes. I took some paracetamol from the medical shop, but it's not helping much.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rakesh Kumar]]: Hello Shanti Devi. Thank you for reaching out. I understand you are having a headache for three days, with nausea, and it's not responding to paracetamol. Can you tell me if you have any fever, or if your vision has changed? Also, have you had any recent head injury?
Patient: No fever, doctor. My vision is normal. I haven't bumped my head or anything. The pain is just there, a constant pressure. I also feel a little dizzy when I stand up too quickly.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rakesh Kumar]]: I see. The dizziness on standing is something we should note. Based on your symptoms, it could be a tension-type headache or a migraine variant. Since you have nausea and dizziness, I want to be cautious. I am advising you to come to the [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sub-District Hospital Reasi]] for a quick check-up. We can do a basic neurological examination and check your blood pressure. Please come tomorrow morning if possible. Your [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] is confirmed.
Patient: Okay doctor. I will try to come tomorrow. What should I do in the meantime?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rakesh Kumar]]: For now, please avoid bending down and try to rest in a quiet, dark room. You can continue taking paracetamol, but do not exceed the daily limit. Drink plenty of water. If the headache becomes severe or you start vomiting, you must come to the hospital immediately. We will see you tomorrow. Take care.
