Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Referral Letter for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Hindi (code=hi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Uttar Pradesh, district=Lucknow.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Hindi.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 42
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Steward, Hotel
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Uttar Pradesh
- district: Lucknow
- first_language: Hindi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 42-year-old female from rural Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, currently married, works as a hotel steward with an illiterate education. She speaks Hindi, primarily Awadhi, and lives in a joint family setting. Clinically, she has limited literacy but can recognize symbols and basic signage, and she practices yoga for joint discomfort.

Document format guide for Referral Letter (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-01]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|36]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Reason: <domain-appropriate clinical reason matching persona>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- REFERRAL_ID — Referral ID e.g. [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PASSPORT_NUMBER — Passport Number e.g. [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
- DRIVING_LICENCE — Driving Licence Number e.g. [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-LKO-001]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Dr. Ram Manohar Lohia Hospital]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Nair]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Savitri Devi]], [[AGE|42]] / [[GENDER|Female]], District [[DISTRICT|Lucknow]]
Reason: Evaluation of a persistent, non-healing ulcer on the left lower leg with suspected malignant transformation, requiring oncological assessment and management.

Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Savitri Devi]] is a 42-year-old female from a rural area of Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, who presents with a chronic ulcer on her left leg for the past eight months. The lesion, initially thought to be a simple wound, has progressively worsened despite topical treatments. The patient reports intermittent pain and occasional bleeding. She has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. Her [[MRN|MRN-2024-LKO-0891]] is on file. She is accompanied by her husband, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Kumar]], who is her primary caregiver.

On examination, a 4x3 cm ulcer is noted on the medial aspect of the left lower leg, with raised, rolled edges and a necrotic base. There is regional lymphadenopathy. Given the clinical suspicion of squamous cell carcinoma, a biopsy is recommended. The patient's [[PHONE_NUMBER|9454321098]] and [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP28 20100012345]] have been verified for contact. Her [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P8765432]] is available for any future travel-related procedures.

Please arrange for a comprehensive oncological workup, including imaging and histopathological confirmation. We request your expert opinion on the staging and definitive treatment plan. The patient's [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]] is registered in the national health system. Her [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|30912345678901]] details are on file for any financial assistance queries. We look forward to your prompt attention to this complex case.

payload.professional_persona: Deepmala Tiwari is a seasoned hotel steward known for their vivid storytelling, adept housekeeping, and inventive 'jugaad' solutions that make every guest feel at home.
payload.linguistic_persona: Deepmala Tiwari speaks fluent Hindi enriched with the Awadhi dialect, understands basic spoken English through gestures and context, and excels at oral storytelling and memorising routines, despite being unable to read or write; they rely on vivid verbal expression to connect with guests.
payload.sports_persona: Deepmala Tiwari follows cricket passionately, cheering for the Lucknow Super Giants, participates in weekend walking clubs along the Gomti River, and they practice yoga each morning to ease joint discomfort, reflecting a balanced approach to fitness.
payload.arts_persona: Deepmala Tiwari revels in Awadhi folk music, devotional bhajans, and Ramlila performances, often humming tunes of Ustad Rashid Khan and Shreya Ghoshal while they stitch intricate Chikankari designs inspired by traditional motifs, showcasing a deep love for cultural arts.
payload.travel_persona: Deepmala Tiwari enjoys weekend trips to Lucknow's Bara Imambara and the historic Taj Mahal in Agra, plans occasional pilgrimages to Varanasi, and they dream of a cultural tour of Jaipur's heritage hotels, balancing careful itinerary planning with spontaneous day-trips.
payload.culinary_persona: Deepmala Tiwari delights in preparing Mishri ka halwa, richly spiced kebabs, and aromatic biryani, blending saffron and cardamom, and they often cook these dishes for guests, though they prefer to follow cherished family recipes rather than experiment.
payload.persona: Deepmala Tiwari is a talkative, imaginative 42-year-old hospitality professional who blends traditional storytelling with practical 'jugaad', dreams of a heritage guesthouse, and spices life with folk music, embroidery, and generous hospitality; they radiate warmth wherever they go.
payload.cultural_background: Deepmala hails from the Awadhi cultural milieu of the rural outskirts of Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh. She grew up in a joint family where traditional North Indian customs, such as celebrating Diwali, Holi, and Navratri, were central. Her community places a high value on hospitality and sharing food, which later influenced her choice of a career in the hotel sector. Even without formal schooling, she inherited a rich oral tradition of folk songs, proverbs and the craft of Chikankari embroidery passed down from her mother and neighbours. Her cultural identity is a blend of rustic village life and the cosmopolitan exposure she gets daily from interacting with guests from diverse backgrounds.
payload.linguistic_background: Deepmala's mother‑tongue is Hindi, and she commonly uses the Awadhi dialect in daily conversation. Being illiterate, she cannot read or write Hindi or English fluently, but she can recognise familiar symbols, hotel signage, and basic numerals. She understands simple spoken English from guests and manages communication through gestures, body language, and contextual cues. Her linguistic strengths lie in oral storytelling, memorising routines, and using vivid, imaginative vocabulary when interacting with guests, which helps her create a warm and engaging atmosphere.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Deepmala excels in hospitality operations, especially in guest relations and housekeeping. She is adept at cleaning rooms, managing linens, serving food and beverages, and ensuring that guests feel welcomed. Her strong oral communication allows her to convey instructions and assist guests without relying on written material. She employs practical "jugaad" solutions to solve on‑the‑spot problems, and she is known for her storytelling ability, which adds a personal touch to guest interactions. Additionally, she possesses good knowledge of local attractions in Lucknow, basic first aid, and the ability to coordinate short‑term tasks among her team members.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Guest relations", "Housekeeping", "Room cleaning", "Linen management", "Food and beverage service", "Storytelling", "Problem solving (jugaad)", "Team coordination", "Knowledge of local attractions", "Basic first aid"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Deepmala enjoys listening to traditional Awadhi folk music and devotional bhajans, attending Ramlila performances during festivals, and exploring Lucknow’s historic sites such as the Bara Imambara and Chota Imambara. She loves cooking and experimenting with regional recipes, especially Mishri ka halwa and kebabs. In her spare time she practices Chikankari embroidery, shares stories with neighbours, sings bhajans, and organises small gatherings where she can interact with friends and guests, reflecting her sociable nature.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to folk music", "Attending Ramlila performances", "Cooking regional dishes", "Chikankari embroidery", "Storytelling with neighbours", "Singing devotional bhajans", "Exploring historic sites in Lucknow", "Socialising with guests and friends"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Deepmala aspires to progress from her current steward role to a supervisory position within the hotel, eventually managing a small team and overseeing guest services. She envisions opening a boutique heritage guesthouse in Lucknow that showcases Awadhi culture, Chikankari crafts and traditional cuisine. In parallel, she wishes to enroll in adult literacy programmes to acquire basic reading and writing skills in Hindi, which will help her maintain records and communicate more effectively with management.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 42-year-old female from rural Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, currently married, works as a hotel steward with an illiterate education. She speaks Hindi, primarily Awadhi dialect, and lives in a joint family setting. Clinically, she may have limited health literacy, potential for hypertension due to dietary habits, and requires communication support for health instructions. Her lifestyle includes traditional storytelling, folk music, and limited physical activity, with possible joint discomfort.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Hindi (code=hi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.

- sex / gender: Female
- age: 42
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Steward, Hotel
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Uttar Pradesh
- district: Lucknow
- first_language: Hindi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 42-year-old female from rural Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, currently married, works as a hotel steward with an illiterate education. She speaks Hindi, primarily Awadhi dialect, and lives in a joint family setting. Clinically, she may have limited health literacy, potential for hypertension due to dietary habits, and requires communication support for health instructions. Her lifestyle includes traditional storytelling, folk music, and limited physical activity, with possible joint discomfort.

Document format guide for Radiology / Imaging Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Imaging report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Female]]

payload.professional_persona: Deepmala Tiwari is a seasoned hotel steward known for their vivid storytelling, adept housekeeping, and inventive 'jugaad' solutions that make every guest feel at home.
payload.linguistic_persona: Deepmala Tiwari speaks fluent Hindi enriched with the Awadhi dialect, understands basic spoken English through gestures and context, and excels at oral storytelling and memorising routines, despite being unable to read or write; they rely on vivid verbal expression to connect with guests.
payload.sports_persona: Deepmala Tiwari follows cricket passionately, cheering for the Lucknow Super Giants, participates in weekend walking clubs along the Gomti River, and they practice yoga each morning to ease joint discomfort, reflecting a balanced approach to fitness.
payload.arts_persona: Deepmala Tiwari revels in Awadhi folk music, devotional bhajans, and Ramlila performances, often humming tunes of Ustad Rashid Khan and Shreya Ghoshal while they stitch intricate Chikankari designs inspired by traditional motifs, showcasing a deep love for cultural arts.
payload.travel_persona: Deepmala Tiwari enjoys weekend trips to Lucknow's Bara Imambara and the historic Taj Mahal in Agra, plans occasional pilgrimages to Varanasi, and they dream of a cultural tour of Jaipur's heritage hotels, balancing careful itinerary planning with spontaneous day-trips.
payload.culinary_persona: Deepmala Tiwari delights in preparing Mishri ka halwa, richly spiced kebabs, and aromatic biryani, blending saffron and cardamom, and they often cook these dishes for guests, though they prefer to follow cherished family recipes rather than experiment.
payload.persona: Deepmala Tiwari is a talkative, imaginative 42-year-old hospitality professional who blends traditional storytelling with practical 'jugaad', dreams of a heritage guesthouse, and spices life with folk music, embroidery, and generous hospitality; they radiate warmth wherever they go.
payload.cultural_background: Deepmala hails from the Awadhi cultural milieu of the rural outskirts of Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh. She grew up in a joint family where traditional North Indian customs, such as celebrating Diwali, Holi, and Navratri, were central. Her community places a high value on hospitality and sharing food, which later influenced her choice of a career in the hotel sector. Even without formal schooling, she inherited a rich oral tradition of folk songs, proverbs and the craft of Chikankari embroidery passed down from her mother and neighbours. Her cultural identity is a blend of rustic village life and the cosmopolitan exposure she gets daily from interacting with guests from diverse backgrounds.
payload.linguistic_background: Deepmala's mother‑tongue is Hindi, and she commonly uses the Awadhi dialect in daily conversation. Being illiterate, she cannot read or write Hindi or English fluently, but she can recognise familiar symbols, hotel signage, and basic numerals. She understands simple spoken English from guests and manages communication through gestures, body language, and contextual cues. Her linguistic strengths lie in oral storytelling, memorising routines, and using vivid, imaginative vocabulary when interacting with guests, which helps her create a warm and engaging atmosphere.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Deepmala excels in hospitality operations, especially in guest relations and housekeeping. She is adept at cleaning rooms, managing linens, serving food and beverages, and ensuring that guests feel welcomed. Her strong oral communication allows her to convey instructions and assist guests without relying on written material. She employs practical "jugaad" solutions to solve on‑the‑spot problems, and she is known for her storytelling ability, which adds a personal touch to guest interactions. Additionally, she possesses good knowledge of local attractions in Lucknow, basic first aid, and the ability to coordinate short‑term tasks among her team members.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Guest relations", "Housekeeping", "Room cleaning", "Linen management", "Food and beverage service", "Storytelling", "Problem solving (jugaad)", "Team coordination", "Knowledge of local attractions", "Basic first aid"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Deepmala enjoys listening to traditional Awadhi folk music and devotional bhajans, attending Ramlila performances during festivals, and exploring Lucknow’s historic sites such as the Bara Imambara and Chota Imambara. She loves cooking and experimenting with regional recipes, especially Mishri ka halwa and kebabs. In her spare time she practices Chikankari embroidery, shares stories with neighbours, sings bhajans, and organises small gatherings where she can interact with friends and guests, reflecting her sociable nature.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to folk music", "Attending Ramlila performances", "Cooking regional dishes", "Chikankari embroidery", "Storytelling with neighbours", "Singing devotional bhajans", "Exploring historic sites in Lucknow", "Socialising with guests and friends"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Deepmala aspires to progress from her current steward role to a supervisory position within the hotel, eventually managing a small team and overseeing guest services. She envisions opening a boutique heritage guesthouse in Lucknow that showcases Awadhi culture, Chikankari crafts and traditional cuisine. In parallel, she wishes to enroll in adult literacy programmes to acquire basic reading and writing skills in Hindi, which will help her maintain records and communicate more effectively with management.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 42-year-old female from rural Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, currently married, works as a hotel steward with an illiterate education. She speaks Hindi, primarily Awadhi, and lives in a joint family setting. Clinically, she has limited literacy but can recognize basic signage and numbers. Her lifestyle includes traditional storytelling, folk music, and embroidery, with a preference for home-cooked meals.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]

  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]

  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kannada (code=kn, script=Kannada). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Andhra Pradesh, district=Anantapur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Kannada for Kannada.

PLACEMENT (OPD header): First line MUST include BOTH [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] (facility name) AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] (facility code) — never substitute one for the other.
Put [[EMPLOYEE_ID|…]] on the registrar/clerk line and [[RELATIVE_NAME|…]] on the attendant/relative line. Include [[DOB|YYYY-MM-DD]] and [[OCCUPATION|…]] with demographics.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 40
- marital_status: Widowed
- occupation: Garbage Collectors, Other
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Andhra Pradesh
- district: Anantapur
- first_language: Kannada
- clinical_persona_summary: A 40-year-old female waste collector from a rural area in Anantapur, Andhra Pradesh, who is widowed and speaks Kannada as her first language. She has an illiterate education level and works in the informal sector, experiencing chronic back pain. Her lifestyle includes a love for folk songs and occasional festival sweets, with limited formal education.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Anantapur]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-AN-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Lakshmi]] | DOB [[DOB|1984-07-22]] | Age: [[AGE|40]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Garbage Collector]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-AN-2024-089]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Reddy]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Savitri]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9440012345]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-AN-042]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Anantapur]]
Chief complaint: Lower back pain and swelling in legs for 2 months, suspected pregnancy-related issue.

History of present illness: Patient is a 40-year-old widowed female from Shivapuram village, Anantapur district. She reports progressive lower back pain for the past two months, which worsens with prolonged standing, a common activity in her occupation. For the last month, she has noticed bilateral pedal edema, which is more pronounced in the evenings. She denies any fever, chills, or recent trauma. She mentions her last menstrual period was approximately 8 weeks ago, and she has missed two cycles. She has not taken any medications for the pain or swelling.

Examination: On examination, the patient is conscious and oriented. Her vitals are stable: BP 130/80 mmHg, Pulse 88/min, Temperature 98.4°F. She is averagely built. On abdominal examination, the uterus is enlarged, consistent with approximately 12 weeks of gestation. There is tenderness on palpation of the lumbar spine. Lower limbs show pitting edema up to the mid-tibia bilaterally.

Investigations: Urine pregnancy test is positive. Hemoglobin is 10.5 g/dL. Blood sugar is 95 mg/dL. Ultrasound abdomen shows a single live intrauterine fetus with a crown-rump length corresponding to 11 weeks and 5 days.

Diagnosis:
1. Pregnancy, 12 weeks gestation
2. Lower back pain
3. Bilateral pedal edema

Plan:
1. Counselled the patient regarding her pregnancy and the importance of antenatal care.
2. Prescribed iron and folic acid tablets once daily.
3. Advised pelvic rest and to avoid heavy lifting.
4. Recommended a follow-up appointment in 4 weeks for routine antenatal check-up.
5. Referred to the physiotherapy department for management of back pain.
6. Instructed to report any vaginal bleeding, severe headache, or decreased fetal movements immediately.

Follow-up: [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-AN-2024-0915]]

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →

GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete PHC Register Entry (Primary Health Centre) for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kannada (code=kn, script=Kannada). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Andhra Pradesh, district=Anantapur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Kannada for Kannada.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 40
- marital_status: Widowed
- occupation: Garbage Collectors, Other
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Andhra Pradesh
- district: Anantapur
- first_language: Kannada
- clinical_persona_summary: A 40-year-old female waste collector from rural Anantapur, Andhra Pradesh, who is widowed and has an illiterate education, speaks Kannada as her first language and understands Hindi. She lives in a rural zone and works in the informal sector, experiencing chronic back pain. Her lifestyle includes community-oriented activities and a cultural affinity for folk music.

Document format guide for PHC Register Entry (Primary Health Centre) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
PHC register — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC ...]] Village [[VILLAGE|...]] District [[DISTRICT|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|19]] [[GENDER|Female]] | brief entry line

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
PHC register — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Primary Health Centre, Anantapur]] Village [[VILLAGE|Kandukur]] District [[DISTRICT|Anantapur]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Lakshmi]] [[AGE|40]] [[GENDER|Female]] | TB screening and NCD review

Patient Lakshmi, a 40-year-old widowed female from Kandukur village, presented for her monthly DOTS therapy and routine NCD screening. She reports persistent dry cough for the past three weeks, which has worsened over the last week. She also complains of mild, intermittent chest pain and progressive weight loss of approximately 3 kilograms in the last two months. She has a known history of hypertension, for which she takes Amlodipine 5mg daily. On examination, her vitals are stable: BP 130/85 mmHg, Pulse 88/min, Respiratory Rate 18/min, Temperature 98.4°F. Chest auscultation reveals diminished breath sounds in the right upper zone. Her blood sugar fasting level was 108 mg/dL. A sputum sample was collected for GeneXpert testing. The patient was counseled on the importance of completing the full course of anti-tubercular therapy and was advised to continue her antihypertensive medication. She was referred to the District Hospital for further evaluation and management of her TB.

[[PHONE_NUMBER|9440123456]]
[[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sujatha]]
[[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-AP-345678-9012]]
[[VOTER_ID|AP123456789]]
[[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

payload.professional_persona: Mani, a resilient waste collector who expertly sorts recyclables using hand‑cart tricks and rallies neighbours with spontaneous community clean‑up drives, they dream of formal training and a women‑run recycling centre that will dignify their work.
payload.linguistic_persona: Mani, a fluent Kannada speaker who understands Hindi but cannot read or write either language, they rely on oral instructions and visual cues to navigate daily tasks.
payload.sports_persona: Mani, an avid cricket enthusiast who follows matches on the village radio, roots for Royal Challengers Bangalore and cheers for the local Andhra women’s cricket team, occasionally joins neighbourhood kabaddi games for fun, and walks briskly to keep fit, they rarely visit a formal gym.
payload.arts_persona: Mani, a passionate lover of folk music who sings along to Kannada legends like Kuttur Mallappa and Telugu troupe Kinnera, joins village bhajan circles, dances to traditional dandiya whenever festivals arrive, and keeps a collection of cassette tapes from her youth, they rarely have time for formal music lessons.
payload.travel_persona: Mani, a modest traveler who takes occasional bus trips to the nearest market town of Ananthapur, the pilgrimage site of Tirumala, and once visited Hyderabad by train to see her niece, they plan journeys spontaneously with family, prefer budget seats and stays with relatives, and dream of a future weekend getaway to the beaches of Visakhapatnam.
payload.culinary_persona: Mani, a home‑cooking enthusiast who prepares ragi mudde with spicy sambar, pulihora, fresh coconut chutney, and tangy mango pickle for neighbours, enjoys seasonal mangoes in salads and desserts, and while she effortlessly whips up these traditional dishes, they rarely experiment beyond the familiar pantry staples.
payload.persona: Mani, a 40‑year‑old waste‑collector who blends spontaneous community‑jugaad with a deep love for folk songs, juggling chronic back ache and a dream of a women‑run recycling hub, they occasionally splurge on festival sweets despite a cash‑only lifestyle.
payload.cultural_background: Mani belongs to a Kannada-speaking community that migrated to the rural outskirts of Anantapur in Andhra Pradesh. Her family has roots in the traditionally agricultural villages of northern Karnataka, but over generations they have settled in the agrarian hinterland of Andhra, adapting to local customs while preserving Kannada folk traditions, especially in food, festivals like Ugadi, and oral storytelling.
payload.linguistic_background: Mani's mother tongue is Kannada, which she speaks fluently and uses for daily communication with neighbours and fellow workers. She also understands and converses in Hindi, the lingua franca of the region, though her literacy in both languages is limited due to illiteracy. She cannot read or write Kannada or Hindi, relying on oral instructions and visual cues for her work.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Mani is adept at manual waste collection, efficiently gathering household garbage from narrow lanes using a hand‑cart. She can quickly sort recyclable materials (plastic, metal, paper) from non‑recyclables, applying simple segregation techniques. Her physical stamina enables her to work long hours in hot weather. She is skilled at community mobilisation, encouraging neighbours to keep streets clean, and uses practical ‘jugaad’ solutions to handle overflowing bins. She also knows basic first‑aid for minor injuries that occur on the job.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Garbage collection", "Waste segregation", "Recycling", "Physical stamina", "Community mobilisation", "Jugaad solutions", "Basic first aid"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Mani loves listening to folk songs in Kannada and Telugu, especially those sung during village festivals, and often joins community dance circles. She enjoys gardening a small patch of vegetables near her house, tending to mango saplings, and cooking traditional meals for neighbours. In her free moments she watches cricket matches on a communal radio, participates in nightly bhajan sessions, and takes pride in experimenting with simple recycling crafts like making reusable cloth bags.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Folk singing", "Traditional dancing", "Vegetable gardening", "Cooking", "Watching cricket on radio", "Community bhajan sessions", "DIY recycling crafts"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Mani aspires to improve the dignity and safety of waste workers in her village. She hopes to receive formal training in waste management and eventually start a small community-run recycling centre that can generate modest income and provide employment to other widows and marginalised women. She also wishes to secure a pension or government welfare benefits for widows, ensuring stable financial support in her later years. Long‑term, she dreams of seeing her village cleaner, with less litter and better waste segregation practices.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 40-year-old female waste collector from a rural, illiterate, Kannada-speaking background in Anantapur, Andhra Pradesh, who is widowed. She works in the informal sector and has limited formal education, relying on community-based solutions for daily challenges. Her lifestyle includes chronic back pain and a cultural affinity for folk songs, with occasional consumption of festival sweets. She demonstrates resilience in her occupation and expresses aspirations for a women-run recycling hub.

- sex / gender: Female
- age: 40
- marital_status: Widowed
- occupation: Garbage Collectors, Other
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Andhra Pradesh
- district: Anantapur
- first_language: Kannada
- clinical_persona_summary: A 40-year-old female waste collector from a rural, illiterate, Kannada-speaking background in Anantapur, Andhra Pradesh, who is widowed. She works in the informal sector and has limited formal education, relying on community-based solutions for daily challenges. - PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.

payload.professional_persona: Mohamad Sheikh, a seasoned heavy‑truck driver, leverages meticulous route‑planning, hands‑on vehicle maintenance and multilingual communication to consistently meet tight delivery schedules while maintaining a calm, adaptable demeanor on congested coastal highways.
payload.linguistic_persona: Mohamad Sheikh, fluent in Konkani and Marathi, speaks Hindi conversationally and reads basic Hindi, while functional English lets them navigate road signs and simple paperwork with confidence.
payload.sports_persona: Mohamad Sheikh, a regular participant in weekend cricket matches with friends at the local Goa Cricket Club and an enthusiastic supporter of FC Goa, keeps fit through occasional jogs along the beach, though they often prioritize a quick nap over a gym session after long drives.
payload.arts_persona: Mohamad Sheikh, an avid follower of Goan folk legends like Remo Fernandes and Lorna, revels in qawwali evenings featuring vocalist Firoz Ali, and spends spare time capturing sunrise silhouettes at Miramar through his DSLR, often drawing inspiration from exhibitions of local painter Antonio Demétrio.
payload.travel_persona: Mohamad Sheikh, a road‑trip enthusiast, enjoys weekend getaways to nearby beaches like Colva and Diu, has trekked the Western Ghats in Mahabaleshwar, and dreams of a cultural tour of Lisbon to soak up the Portuguese legacy, always balancing careful budgeting with a love for spontaneous coastal drives.
payload.culinary_persona: Mohamad Sheikh, a modest home cook, loves preparing Goan fish curry with kokum and coconut milk, sizzling prawn balchão, and savoring freshly caught pomfret, while often sprinkling extra chilies that sometimes leave his palate burning.
payload.persona: Mohamad Sheikh, a diligent trucker with a photographer’s eye, blends disciplined routes with a love for coastal art, occasional spice‑overload, and a steady plan to turn his wheels into a future logistics empire.
payload.cultural_background: Mohamad Sheikh belongs to the small but vibrant Muslim community in South Goa, a region where Konkani is the mother tongue and the Portuguese colonial legacy blends with Indian traditions. Growing up in an urban coastal town, he has been exposed to a mix of Goan Catholic and Muslim cultural practices, enjoys local festivals, seafood cuisine and the multilingual ambience of the area.
payload.linguistic_background: Mohamad is fluent in Konkani, his first language, and can comfortably read and write it using the Devanagari script. He also speaks Marathi fluently, communicating effortlessly in daily interactions and can read Marathi with moderate proficiency. Hindi, learned as his third language, is well understood for spoken communication and basic reading, though his writing skills are limited. Due to his occupation, he possesses functional English literacy sufficient for reading road signs, basic forms, and simple instructions.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Mohamad has extensive expertise in operating heavy trucks and lorries, including maneuvering large vehicles through congested urban roads and coastal highways. He is proficient in vehicle inspection, routine maintenance, and troubleshooting mechanical issues on the spot. His knowledge of route planning, logistics coordination, and compliance with transport regulations ensures timely deliveries. He also possesses strong communication skills in Konkani, Marathi, and basic Hindi, enabling effective interaction with diverse clients and dispatchers.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Heavy truck operation", "Lorry driving", "Vehicle inspection and maintenance", "Mechanical troubleshooting", "Route planning", "Logistics coordination", "Transport regulations compliance", "Multilingual communication (Konkani, Marathi, Hindi)", "Time management", "Customer service"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: In his free time, Mohamad enjoys listening to Goan folk music and qawwali, visiting local art exhibitions, and capturing coastal scenery through photography. He loves cooking traditional Goan seafood dishes, playing cricket with friends, and reading short stories in Konkani. His curiosity also leads him to experiment with simple DIY repairs on his truck and follow online forums about vehicle technology.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Goan folk music and qawwali", "Visiting art exhibitions", "Coastal photography", "Cooking Goan seafood dishes", "Playing cricket with friends", "Reading Konkani short stories", "DIY truck maintenance", "Following vehicle technology forums", "Exploring local history"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: As a truck driver, Mohamad aspires to become a senior fleet manager, overseeing a team of drivers and eventually launching his own logistics enterprise in Goa. He plans to obtain advanced driver certifications, master modern fleet management software, and increase his earnings to secure financial stability for a future family. He also wishes to pursue his curiosity for art and culture during off‑hours, perhaps taking short courses in photography or local history.
payload.occupation: Heavy Truck and Lorry Drivers, Other
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Mohamad Sheikh is a 30-year-old male, never married, working as a heavy truck driver in South Goa, with a literate education level. He is fluent in Konkani and Marathi, living in an urban setting. His lifestyle includes a sedentary job with occasional long-distance driving, a diet high in spicy Goan cuisine, and limited physical activity.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]

  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]

  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]

  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:

GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:

GOOD:

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →

GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]

  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]

  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]

  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Insurance Claim / TPA Form for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Konkani (code=kok, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Goa, district=South Goa.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Konkani.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once (parking/ambulance or motor claim line). Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 30
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Heavy Truck and Lorry Drivers, Other
- education_level: Literate without education level
- zone: Urban
- state: Goa
- district: South Goa
- first_language: Konkani
- clinical_persona_summary: Mohamad Sheikh, a 30-year-old male, is a heavy truck driver in South Goa, never married, with a literate education level. He resides in an urban area and speaks Konkani as his first language, with fluency in Marathi. His lifestyle includes a sedentary occupation with limited physical activity and a diet high in spicy foods.

Document format guide for Insurance Claim / TPA Form (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
TPA claim — Policy [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|25]] [[GENDER|Male]] Aadhaar [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Motor / RTA vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
PAN [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]] IFSC [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]] account [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|...]]

MANDATORY inline entities (16 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER — Insurance Policy Number e.g. [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
- AADHAAR_NUMBER — Aadhaar Number e.g. [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PAN_NUMBER — PAN Number e.g. [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
- BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER — Bank Account Number e.g. [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
- IFSC_CODE — IFSC Code e.g. [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
- BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER — Bank Routing Number e.g. [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
- CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER — Credit Card Number e.g. [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
- CVV — CVV e.g. [[CVV|123]]
- PIN — PIN e.g. [[PIN|4829]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

PAN: [[PAN_NUMBER|SHFM1234C]]

Policy Number: [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-GOA-2024-9876]]
Bank Account: [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|30912345678901]]
IFSC: [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0000123]]
Bank Routing: [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0000123]]
Credit Card: [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|5555555555554444]]
CVV: [[CVV|456]]
PIN: [[PIN|4321]]

Hospital Details:
Name: [[HOSPITAL_NAME|South Goa District Hospital]]
District: [[DISTRICT|South Goa]]
Admission Number: ADM-2024-1015-004
MRN: MRN-SGH-2024-0015
Encounter ID: ENC-2024-1015-004

Clinical Summary:
Chief Complaint: Persistent cough for three weeks with intermittent low-grade fever and night sweats. Patient reports increasing fatigue and weight loss of approximately five kilograms over the past month. He attributes the symptoms to his heavy-duty driving job and irregular sleep patterns.

History of Present Illness:
Mohamad Sheikh, a 30-year-old male heavy truck driver, presents with a three-week history of a productive cough, initially dry and later with scanty sputum. He experiences low-grade fever in the evenings and drenching night sweats. He reports progressive fatigue and a five-kilogram weight loss. He denies hemoptysis, chest pain, or shortness of breath. He mentions a history of smoking bidis for the past ten years, approximately ten per day.

Past Medical History:
No known chronic illnesses. No prior hospitalizations. No known allergies.

Physical Examination:
General: Thin, cachectic male, appears fatigued.
Vital Signs: BP 120/80 mmHg, Pulse 98/min, Respiratory Rate 18/min, Temperature 37.8°C, SpO2 96% on room air.
Chest: Bilateral breath sounds with fine inspiratory crackles noted in the left upper zone. No wheezing or rhonchi. No signs of clubbing.

Investigations:
Chest X-ray: Shows a left upper lobe infiltrate with cavitation, suggestive of pulmonary tuberculosis.
Sputum for AFB: Positive.
Sputum for GeneXpert: Positive for Mycobacterium tuberculosis complex, resistant to none of the first-line drugs.
Complete Blood Count: Hemoglobin 10.5 g/dL, WBC count 8,000/cumm with neutrophilia.
Liver and Kidney Function Tests: Within normal limits.

Diagnosis:
Pulmonary Tuberculosis, newly diagnosed, left upper lobe.

Treatment Plan:
Started on Category I DOTS regimen as per National Tuberculosis Elimination Programme guidelines.
Isoniazid 300 mg daily, Rifampicin 450 mg daily, Pyrazinamide 1.5 g daily, Ethambutol 750 mg daily for two months, followed by Isoniazid and Rifampicin for four months.
Patient counseled on treatment adherence and side effects.
Referred to the District TB Officer for directly observed therapy.

Prognosis:
Good, provided treatment adherence is maintained.

Billing Summary:
Total estimated cost for two months of anti-tubercular therapy and initial investigations is fifteen thousand rupees. The claim is submitted for reimbursement under the policy.

Declaration:
I hereby declare that the above information is true and correct to the best of my knowledge and belief.

Doctor's Signature: Dr. Anjali Desai
Date: 15 October 2024

payload.professional_persona: Mohamad Sheikh, a seasoned heavy‑truck driver, leverages meticulous route‑planning, hands‑on vehicle maintenance and multilingual communication to consistently meet tight delivery schedules while maintaining a calm, adaptable demeanor on congested coastal highways.
payload.linguistic_persona: Mohamad Sheikh, fluent in Konkani and Marathi, speaks Hindi conversationally and reads basic Hindi, while functional English lets them navigate road signs and simple paperwork with confidence.
payload.sports_persona: Mohamad Sheikh, a regular participant in weekend cricket matches with friends at the local Goa Cricket Club and an enthusiastic supporter of FC Goa, keeps fit through occasional jogs along the beach, though they often prioritize a quick nap over a gym session after long drives.
payload.arts_persona: Mohamad Sheikh, an avid follower of Goan folk legends like Remo Fernandes and Lorna, revels in qawwali evenings featuring vocalist Firoz Ali, and spends spare time capturing sunrise silhouettes at Miramar through his DSLR, often drawing inspiration from exhibitions of local painter Antonio Demétrio.
payload.travel_persona: Mohamad Sheikh, a road‑trip enthusiast, enjoys weekend getaways to nearby beaches like Colva and Diu, has trekked the Western Ghats in Mahabaleshwar, and dreams of a cultural tour of Lisbon to soak up the Portuguese legacy, always balancing careful budgeting with a love for spontaneous coastal drives.
payload.culinary_persona: Mohamad Sheikh, a modest home cook, loves preparing Goan fish curry with kokum and coconut milk, sizzling prawn balchão, and savoring freshly caught pomfret, while often sprinkling extra chilies that sometimes leave his palate burning.
payload.persona: Mohamad Sheikh, a diligent trucker with a photographer’s eye, blends disciplined routes with a love for coastal art, occasional spice‑overload, and a steady plan to turn his wheels into a future logistics empire.
payload.cultural_background: Mohamad Sheikh belongs to the small but vibrant Muslim community in South Goa, a region where Konkani is the mother tongue and the Portuguese colonial legacy blends with Indian traditions. Growing up in an urban coastal town, he has been exposed to a mix of Goan Catholic and Muslim cultural practices, enjoys local festivals, seafood cuisine and the multilingual ambience of the area.
payload.linguistic_background: Mohamad is fluent in Konkani, his first language, and can comfortably read and write it using the Devanagari script. He also speaks Marathi fluently, communicating effortlessly in daily interactions and can read Marathi with moderate proficiency. Hindi, learned as his third language, is well understood for spoken communication and basic reading, though his writing skills are limited. Due to his occupation, he possesses functional English literacy sufficient for reading road signs, basic forms, and simple instructions.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Mohamad has extensive expertise in operating heavy trucks and lorries, including maneuvering large vehicles through congested urban roads and coastal highways. He is proficient in vehicle inspection, routine maintenance, and troubleshooting mechanical issues on the spot. His knowledge of route planning, logistics coordination, and compliance with transport regulations ensures timely deliveries. He also possesses strong communication skills in Konkani, Marathi, and basic Hindi, enabling effective interaction with diverse clients and dispatchers.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Heavy truck operation", "Lorry driving", "Vehicle inspection and maintenance", "Mechanical troubleshooting", "Route planning", "Logistics coordination", "Transport regulations compliance", "Multilingual communication (Konkani, Marathi, Hindi)", "Time management", "Customer service"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: In his free time, Mohamad enjoys listening to Goan folk music and qawwali, visiting local art exhibitions, and capturing coastal scenery through photography. He loves cooking traditional Goan seafood dishes, playing cricket with friends, and reading short stories in Konkani. His curiosity also leads him to experiment with simple DIY repairs on his truck and follow online forums about vehicle technology.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Goan folk music and qawwali", "Visiting art exhibitions", "Coastal photography", "Cooking Goan seafood dishes", "Playing cricket with friends", "Reading Konkani short stories", "DIY truck maintenance", "Following vehicle technology forums", "Exploring local history"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: As a truck driver, Mohamad aspires to become a senior fleet manager, overseeing a team of drivers and eventually launching his own logistics enterprise in Goa. He plans to obtain advanced driver certifications, master modern fleet management software, and increase his earnings to secure financial stability for a future family. He also wishes to pursue his curiosity for art and culture during off‑hours, perhaps taking short courses in photography or local history.

payload.persona_clinical_summary: Mohamad Sheikh is a 30-year-old male, never married, working as a heavy truck driver in urban South Goa. He is literate without a formal education level and is fluent in Konkani, his first language, and also speaks Marathi. His lifestyle includes a sedentary job with occasional long-distance driving, and he consumes a diet high in chilies and fish.
