payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Hospital Billing / Invoice for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Konkani (code=kok, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Goa, district=South Goa.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Konkani.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once (parking/ambulance or motor claim line). Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 30
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Heavy Truck and Lorry Drivers, Other
- education_level: Literate without education level
- zone: Urban
- state: Goa
- district: South Goa
- first_language: Konkani
- clinical_persona_summary: Mohamad Sheikh is a 30-year-old male, never married, working as a heavy truck driver in urban South Goa. He is literate without a formal education level and is fluent in Konkani, his first language, and also speaks Marathi. His lifestyle includes a sedentary job with occasional long-distance driving, and he consumes a diet high in chilies and fish.

Document format guide for Hospital Billing / Invoice (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Invoice — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] | Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | MRN [[MRN|...]]
Address district [[DISTRICT|...]] PIN [[PIN_CODE|110001]]
Ambulance / parking vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
Line items with amounts (no entity tags on procedure names).

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PIN_CODE — ZIP / Postal Code (PIN Code) e.g. [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- AADHAAR_NUMBER — Aadhaar Number e.g. [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
- INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER — Insurance Policy Number e.g. [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
- TAX_ID — Tax Identification Number (TIN/GSTIN) e.g. [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
- TELEPHONE_LANDLINE — Telephone Number (Landline) e.g. [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

payload.professional_persona: Siraya Begam, a 25‑year‑old deep‑sea fisherwoman, expertly navigates the Arabian Sea, maintains and repairs large fishing nets and boat engines, and they leverage their talkative, sociable nature to coordinate crew and negotiate fair prices for the catch.
payload.linguistic_persona: Siraya Begam speaks fluently in Kashmiri, handles everyday conversations in Hindi, grasps basic English nautical terms, and despite being illiterate, they excel at oral storytelling, memorising prayer verses, and recognising simple signs in Devanagari and Perso‑Arabic scripts.
payload.sports_persona: Siraya Begam keeps fit by swimming in Dal Lake, jogging along its promenade, playing occasional cricket with friends at the local Srinagar United Cricket Club, and they cheer for Real Kashmir FC during football season, while also practicing yoga for flexibility.
payload.arts_persona: Siraya Begam loves listening to the soulful Sufi qawwalis of Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan and the melodious Kashmiri folk ballads of Mahajla, attends local cultural evenings where the sarangi is played, and they spend evenings carving wooden toys inspired by the designs of local artisan craftsmen.
payload.travel_persona: Siraya Begam enjoys spontaneous weekend getaways, often exploring nearby hill stations like Gulmarg and Pahalgam, has taken a short trip to Delhi’s fish market to source new varieties, and they plan to voyage to Kerala’s coastal towns and Goa’s beaches to learn sustainable fishing practices, while preferring budget-friendly buses and shared rides over luxury travel.
payload.culinary_persona: Siraya Begam relishes the rich flavors of their homemade Kashmiri fish curry, simmered with mustard oil, fennel seeds, dry red chilies, and a pinch of garam masala, delights in sipping kahwa infused with saffron and almonds, and they often enjoy crunchy pakoras made from fresh eggplant, though they rarely cook for themselves, preferring to share meals with the crew.
payload.persona: Siraya Begam is a charismatic 25‑year‑old fisherwoman who blends a love for sea‑borne storytelling with a restless curiosity for sustainable tech, and they juggle nightly prayers while indulging in an occasional weakness for scented candles.
payload.cultural_background: Siraya Begam hails from the vibrant Kashmiri community of Srinagar, an urban hub in the scenic valleys of Jammu & Kashmir. As a Muslim woman from the traditional fisherfolk of the Jhelum river and nearby seas, her cultural identity is woven from the rich tapestry of Kashmiri language, Sufi music, Pashmina weaving motifs, and the collective ethos of the local fishing cooperatives. Growing up amidst the lilting thaats of Kashmiri folk songs and the bustle of the local fish market, Siraya embodies the blend of traditional Kashmiri hospitality, modesty rooted in Islamic values, and the pragmatic resilience that characterises the deep‑sea fishing community.
payload.linguistic_background: Linguistically, Siraya’s mother tongue is Kashmiri, which she speaks fluently and uses for all daily communication within her family and the fishing crew. She has functional listening and speaking skills in Hindi, picked up through market interactions, and can understand basic English nautical terms such as ‘net’, ‘boat’, and ‘weather’, often heard from fellow fishermen or radio broadcasts. Siraya is illiterate, meaning she cannot read or write in any language, but she excels at oral storytelling, memorising prayer verses and trade instructions, and she can recognise simple signs in Devanagari and Perso‑Arabic scripts.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Siraya possesses hands‑on expertise in deep‑sea fishing operations. She is adept at navigating the open waters of the Arabian Sea using traditional knowledge of currents, tides, and weather patterns. She skillfully casts, repairs and maintains large fishing nets, and can identify a wide range of fish species common to the region. Her technical abilities extend to maintaining boat engines, handling rope and rigging, and ensuring safety protocols are followed on board. Moreover, her strong interpersonal skills enable effective coordination with crew members, quick decision‑making during emergencies, and community outreach to market the catch.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Deep sea navigation', 'Net casting and repair', 'Marine weather forecasting', 'Fish species identification', 'Boat engine maintenance', 'Rope and rigging handling', 'Safety protocol enforcement', 'Crew coordination', 'Community market outreach', 'Oral storytelling']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Siraya enjoys exploring various forms of art and culture. She often visits local tea houses to listen to Kashmiri folk songs and Sufi qawwalis, and she practices the traditional “Ropar” dance during festivals. In her downtime, she loves crafting simple wooden toys for children, cooking aromatic fish curries, and sharing oral stories of sea voyages with her peers. She is also fascinated by photography, using a basic mobile camera to capture sunrise over Dal Lake and the vibrant life aboard the fishing boat.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Kashmiri folk music', 'Dancing Ropar during festivals', 'Crafting wooden toys', 'Cooking fish curries', 'Sharing oral sea stories', 'Mobile photography of sunrise and boats', 'Visiting tea houses', 'Listening to Sufi qawwalis']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Siraya aspires to modernise her deep‑sea fishing practices by learning sustainable techniques and acquiring basic literacy to manage catch records and market transactions. She aims to establish a women‑led fishing cooperative that empowers fellow female fishers, negotiates better prices, and promotes eco‑friendly gear. Over the next five years, she hopes to obtain a certification in marine safety, upgrade her boat with a more efficient engine, and travel to coastal ports in Kerala and Goa to learn from other fishing communities. Ultimately, she envisions becoming a mentor who blends traditional Kashmiri maritime heritage with contemporary practices, while also supporting local art initiatives.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 25-year-old female from Srinagar, Jammu & Kashmir, works as a deep-sea fisherwoman with an illiterate education level. She is never married, speaks Kashmiri as her first language, and lives in an urban zone. Her lifestyle includes swimming in Dal Lake and practicing Sufi qawwali.

payload.professional_persona: Siraya Begam, a 25‑year‑old deep‑sea fisherwoman, expertly navigates the Arabian Sea, maintains and repairs large fishing nets and boat engines, and they leverage their talkative, sociable nature to coordinate crew and negotiate fair prices for the catch.
payload.linguistic_persona: Siraya Begam speaks fluently in Kashmiri, handles everyday conversations in Hindi, grasps basic English nautical terms, and despite being illiterate, they excel at oral storytelling, memorising prayer verses, and recognising simple signs in Devanagari and Perso‑Arabic scripts.
payload.sports_persona: Siraya Begam keeps fit by swimming in Dal Lake, jogging along its promenade, playing occasional cricket with friends at the local Srinagar United Cricket Club, and they cheer for Real Kashmir FC during football season, while also practicing yoga for flexibility.
payload.arts_persona: Siraya Begam loves listening to the soulful Sufi qawwalis of Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan and the melodious Kashmiri folk ballads of Mahajla, attends local cultural evenings where the sarangi is played, and they spend evenings carving wooden toys inspired by the designs of local artisan craftsmen.
payload.travel_persona: Siraya Begam enjoys spontaneous weekend getaways, often exploring nearby hill stations like Gulmarg and Pahalgam, has taken a short trip to Delhi’s fish market to source new varieties, and they plan to voyage to Kerala’s coastal towns and Goa’s beaches to learn sustainable fishing practices, while preferring budget-friendly buses and shared rides over luxury travel.
payload.culinary_persona: Siraya Begam relishes the rich flavors of their homemade Kashmiri fish curry, simmered with mustard oil, fennel seeds, dry red chilies, and a pinch of garam masala, delights in sipping kahwa infused with saffron and almonds, and they often enjoy crunchy pakoras made from fresh eggplant, though they rarely cook for themselves, preferring to share meals with the crew.
payload.persona: Siraya Begam is a charismatic 25‑year‑old fisherwoman who blends a love for sea‑borne storytelling with a restless curiosity for sustainable tech, and they juggle nightly prayers while indulging in an occasional weakness for scented candles.
payload.cultural_background: Siraya Begam hails from the vibrant Kashmiri community of Srinagar, an urban hub in the scenic valleys of Jammu & Kashmir. As a Muslim woman from the traditional fisherfolk of the Jhelum river and nearby seas, her cultural identity is woven from the rich tapestry of Kashmiri language, Sufi music, Pashmina weaving motifs, and the collective ethos of the local fishing cooperatives. Growing up amidst the lilting thaats of Kashmiri folk songs and the bustle of the local fish market, Siraya embodies the blend of traditional Kashmiri hospitality, modesty rooted in Islamic values, and the pragmatic resilience that characterises the deep‑sea fishing community.
payload.linguistic_background: Linguistically, Siraya’s mother tongue is Kashmiri, which she speaks fluently and uses for all daily communication within her family and the fishing crew. She has functional listening and speaking skills in Hindi, picked up through market interactions, and can understand basic English nautical terms such as ‘net’, ‘boat’, and ‘weather’, often heard from fellow fishermen or radio broadcasts. Siraya is illiterate, meaning she cannot read or write in any language, but she excels at oral storytelling, memorising prayer verses and trade instructions, and she can recognise simple signs in Devanagari and Perso‑Arabic scripts.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Siraya possesses hands‑on expertise in deep‑sea fishing operations. She is adept at navigating the open waters of the Arabian Sea using traditional knowledge of currents, tides, and weather patterns. She skillfully casts, repairs and maintains large fishing nets, and can identify a wide range of fish species common to the region. Her technical abilities extend to maintaining boat engines, handling rope and rigging, and ensuring safety protocols are followed on board. Moreover, her strong interpersonal skills enable effective coordination with crew members, quick decision‑making during emergencies, and community outreach to market the catch.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Deep sea navigation', 'Net casting and repair', 'Marine weather forecasting', 'Fish species identification', 'Boat engine maintenance', 'Rope and rigging handling', 'Safety protocol enforcement', 'Crew coordination', 'Community market outreach', 'Oral storytelling']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Siraya enjoys exploring various forms of art and culture. She often visits local tea houses to listen to Kashmiri folk songs and Sufi qawwalis, and she practices the traditional “Ropar” dance during festivals. In her downtime, she loves crafting simple wooden toys for children, cooking aromatic fish curries, and sharing oral stories of sea voyages with her peers. She is also fascinated by photography, using a basic mobile camera to capture sunrise over Dal Lake and the vibrant life aboard the fishing boat.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Kashmiri folk music', 'Dancing Ropar during festivals', 'Crafting wooden toys', 'Cooking fish curries', 'Sharing oral sea stories', 'Mobile photography of sunrise and boats', 'Visiting tea houses', 'Listening to Sufi qawwalis']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Siraya aspires to modernise her deep‑sea fishing practices by learning sustainable techniques and acquiring basic literacy to manage catch records and market transactions. She aims to establish a women‑led fishing cooperative that empowers fellow female fishers, negotiates better prices, and promotes eco‑friendly gear. Over the next five years, she hopes to obtain a certification in marine safety, upgrade her boat with a more efficient engine, and travel to coastal ports in Kerala and Goa to learn from other fishing communities. Ultimately, she envisions becoming a mentor who blends traditional Kashmiri maritime heritage with contemporary practices, while also supporting local art initiatives.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 25-year-old female from Srinagar, Jammu & Kashmir, who is never married and works as a deep-sea fisherwoman with an illiterate education. Her first language is Kashmiri, and she has functional Hindi skills. She lives in an urban zone and may have limited access to formal healthcare.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags

  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →

GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +

  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.

payload.persona: Siraya Begam is a charismatic 25‑year‑old fisherwoman who blends a love for sea‑borne storytelling with a restless curiosity for sustainable tech, and they juggle nightly prayers while indulging in an occasional weakness for scented candles.
payload.cultural_background: Siraya Begam hails from the vibrant Kashmiri community of Srinagar, an urban hub in the scenic valleys of Jammu & Kashmir. As a Muslim woman from the traditional fisherfolk of the Jhelum river and nearby seas, her cultural identity is woven from the rich tapestry of Kashmiri language, Sufi music, Pashmina weaving motifs, and the collective ethos of the local fishing cooperatives. Growing up amidst the lilting thaats of Kashmiri folk songs and the bustle of the local fish market, Siraya embodies the blend of traditional Kashmiri hospitality, modesty rooted in Islamic values, and the pragmatic resilience that characterises the deep‑sea fishing community.
payload.linguistic_background: Linguistically, Siraya’s mother tongue is Kashmiri, which she speaks fluently and uses for all daily communication within her family and the fishing crew. She has functional listening and speaking skills in Hindi, picked up through market interactions, and can understand basic English nautical terms such as ‘net’, ‘boat’, and ‘weather’, often heard from fellow fishermen or radio broadcasts. Siraya is illiterate, meaning she cannot read or write in any language, but she excels at oral storytelling, memorising prayer verses and trade instructions, and she can recognise simple signs in Devanagari and Perso‑Arabic scripts.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Siraya possesses hands‑on expertise in deep‑sea fishing operations. She is adept at navigating the open waters of the Arabian Sea using traditional knowledge of currents, tides, and weather patterns. She skillfully casts, repairs and maintains large fishing nets, and can identify a wide range of fish species common to the region. Her technical abilities extend to maintaining boat engines, handling rope and rigging, and ensuring safety protocols are followed on board. Moreover, her strong interpersonal skills enable effective coordination with crew members, quick decision‑making during emergencies, and community outreach to market the catch.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Deep sea navigation', 'Net casting and repair', 'Marine weather forecasting', 'Fish species identification', 'Boat engine maintenance', 'Rope and rigging handling', 'Safety protocol enforcement', 'Crew coordination', 'Community market outreach', 'Oral storytelling']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Siraya enjoys exploring various forms of art and culture. She often visits local tea houses to listen to Kashmiri folk songs and Sufi qawwalis, and she practices the traditional “Ropar” dance during festivals. In her downtime, she loves crafting simple wooden toys for children, cooking aromatic fish curries, and sharing oral stories of sea voyages with her peers. She is also fascinated by photography, using a basic mobile camera to capture sunrise over Dal Lake and the vibrant life aboard the fishing boat.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Kashmiri folk music', 'Dancing Ropar during festivals', 'Crafting wooden toys', 'Cooking fish curries', 'Sharing oral sea stories', 'Mobile photography of sunrise and boats', 'Visiting tea houses', 'Listening to Sufi qawwalis']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Siraya aspires to modernise her deep‑sea fishing practices by learning sustainable techniques and acquiring basic literacy to manage catch records and market transactions. She aims to establish a women‑led fishing cooperative that empowers fellow female fishers, negotiates better prices, and promotes eco‑friendly gear. Over the next five years, she hopes to obtain a certification in marine safety, upgrade her boat with a more efficient engine, and travel to coastal ports in Kerala and Goa to learn from other fishing communities. Ultimately, she envisions becoming a mentor who blends traditional Kashmiri maritime heritage with contemporary practices, while also supporting local art initiatives.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 25-year-old female from Srinagar, Jammu & Kashmir, who is never married and works as a deep-sea fisherwoman. She is illiterate, speaks Kashmiri as her first language, and has functional Hindi skills. Her lifestyle includes swimming in Dal Lake and occasional cricket, with a cultural background rooted in the Kashmiri Muslim fisherfolk community.
payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]
  [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
  [[CASTE|OBC]]
  [[CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER|4111111111111111]]
  [[CVV|123]]
  [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
  [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
  [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
  [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]
  [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
  [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]
  [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
  [[GENDER|Female]]
  [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
  [[IFSC_CODE|SBIN0001234]]
  [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
  [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]
  [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
  [[PAN_NUMBER|ABCDE1234F]]
  [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
  [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
  [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
  [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
  [[PIN|4829]]
  [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
  [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
  [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
  [[RELIGION|Hindu]]
  [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
  [[STUDENT_ID|STU-2024-0912]]
  [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
  [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
  [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete ASHA Worker Note for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Kashmiri (code=ks, script=Arabic). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Jammu & Kashmir, district=Srinagar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Arabic for Kashmiri.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 25
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Fisherman, Deep Sea
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Urban
- state: Jammu & Kashmir
- district: Srinagar
- first_language: Kashmiri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 25-year-old female from Srinagar, Jammu & Kashmir, who is never married and works as a deep-sea fisherwoman. She is illiterate, speaks Kashmiri as her first language, and has functional Hindi skills. Her lifestyle includes swimming in Dal Lake and occasional cricket, with a cultural background rooted in the Kashmiri Muslim fisherfolk community.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|...]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|26]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|...]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Visit findings: <2-4 short sentences>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- RELIGION — Religion e.g. [[RELIGION|Hindu]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|Dal Lake]], District [[DISTRICT|Srinagar]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|Ayesha Begum]], [[AGE|25]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Shabnam Bano]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Visit findings: Patient reports persistent cough with fever for three days. She feels weakness and body ache. No known allergies. Mother [[RELATIVE_NAME|Zainab Begum]] accompanies her. BPL card [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-JK-2024-001234]] is presented. Voter ID [[VOTER_ID|JK1234567]] verified. Religion [[RELIGION|Muslim]].

payload.professional_persona: Subhash Chaudhary, a seasoned loader and unloader with over two decades of experience, combines meticulous cargo arrangement skills with a curious eye for valuable Madhubani artwork, and they are regarded as a reliable yet innovative asset in the local logistics hub.
payload.linguistic_persona: Subhash Chaudhary, whose mother tongue is Maithili, speaks fluent spoken Hindi for daily trade, learns new vocabulary via radio and community storytelling, and they can decipher common symbols and signs despite being illiterate.
payload.sports_persona: Subhash Chaudhary follows cricket passionately, cheering for the Kolkata Knight Riders and the Bihar cricket team on the communal TV, and they maintain their fitness by taking early morning walks along the Kosi River banks.
payload.arts_persona: Subhash Chaudhary, an avid admirer of Madhubani art, often discusses intricate motifs with local artist Shanti Devi, listens to Maithili folk singer Sharda Sinha, and they occasionally help select canvases that could fetch higher prices during transport.
payload.travel_persona: Subhash Chaudhary prefers short, affordable trips, having visited the historic city of Varanasi and the serene banks of the Ganga, while they dream of a weekend pilgrimage to Puri and a future road trip to the Himalayan foothills.
payload.culinary_persona: Subhash Chaudhary enjoys hearty meals of litti‑chokha, sattu paratha, and fish curry with mustard seeds, prefers the tangy flavor of raw mango pickle, and they often dine at the local dhaba where they also chat about art and logistics.
payload.persona: Subhash Chaudhary is a diligent logistics worker with a quiet love for Madhubani art, modest savings, and a regular habit of watching cricket, yet they occasionally splurge on a new TV set, reflecting their blend of practicality and quiet indulgence.
payload.cultural_background: Subhash belongs to the Maithil community of Supaul in rural Bihar. His daily life is shaped by the agrarian rhythms, close‑knit family ties, and the vibrant Mithila heritage that includes folk songs, seasonal festivals, and the famous Madhubani art form. He participates in village gatherings, observes local customs around marriage and harvest, and values the community’s emphasis on hospitality and collective support.
payload.linguistic_background: Subhash speaks Maithili as his mother tongue and is fluent in spoken Hindi, which he uses for daily transactions and communicating with traders. Being illiterate, he cannot read or write in any language, but he can recognize common symbols and signs, and he learns new words through listening to radio programmes and community storytelling.
payload.skills_and_expertise: With more than twenty years of experience as a loader and unloader, Subhash is adept at heavy lifting, arranging cargo for optimal weight distribution, and coordinating with truck drivers to ensure timely loading. He has practical knowledge of basic mechanical troubleshooting for equipment, understands local market schedules, and uses his curiosity for art to identify valuable items during transport. His reliability and organized approach make him a trusted worker in the supply chain.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Heavy lifting", "Cargo weight distribution", "Coordination with truck drivers", "Basic mechanical troubleshooting", "Local market schedule knowledge", "Madhubani art appreciation"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: In his free time, Subhash enjoys listening to Maithili folk songs, watching cricket matches on the village’s shared TV, and walking along the banks of the Kosi River. He has a keen eye for Madhubani patterns and often spends evenings discussing the designs with local artisans. He also likes gardening, growing seasonal vegetables, and reading aloud community newsletters circulated by the panchayat.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Maithili folk songs", "Watching cricket on village TV", "Walking by the Kosi River", "Discussing Madhubani designs with artisans", "Gardening seasonal vegetables", "Reading aloud community newsletters"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Subhash aspires to move from manual loading to a supervisory role in the local logistics hub, eventually overseeing a small team of loaders. He wishes to acquire basic literacy through an adult education program, enabling him to keep simple records and read work orders. In the longer term, he dreams of starting a modest transport service that can support his family and contribute to community development, while also promoting Mithila art by sponsoring local Madhubani workshops.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Mr. Subhash Chaudhary, a 45-year-old male loader and unloader, is married and resides in a rural zone of Supaul, Bihar. He is illiterate, speaks Maithili as his first language, and has limited education. His lifestyle includes a modest income, a habit of watching cricket, and occasional purchases of household items.

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Prescription for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Maithili (code=mai, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Bihar, district=Supaul.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Maithili.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 45
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Loader and Unloader
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Bihar
- district: Supaul
- first_language: Maithili
- clinical_persona_summary: Mr. Subhash Chaudhary, a 45-year-old male loader and unloader, is married and resides in a rural zone of Supaul, Bihar. He is illiterate, speaks Maithili as his first language, and has limited education. His lifestyle includes a modest income, a habit of watching cricket, and occasional purchases of household items.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

payload.professional_persona: Subhash Chaudhary, a seasoned loader and unloader with over two decades of experience, combines meticulous cargo arrangement skills with a curious eye for valuable Madhubani artwork, and they are regarded as a reliable yet innovative asset in the local logistics hub.
payload.linguistic_persona: Subhash Chaudhary, whose mother tongue is Maithili, speaks fluent spoken Hindi for daily trade, learns new vocabulary via radio and community storytelling, and they can decipher common symbols and signs despite being illiterate.
payload.sports_persona: Subhash Chaudhary follows cricket passionately, cheering for the Kolkata Knight Riders and the Bihar cricket team on the communal TV, and they maintain their fitness by taking early morning walks along the Kosi River banks.
payload.arts_persona: Subhash Chaudhary, an avid admirer of Madhubani art, often discusses intricate motifs with local artist Shanti Devi, listens to Maithili folk singer Sharda Sinha, and they occasionally help select canvases that could fetch higher prices during transport.
payload.travel_persona: Subhash Chaudhary prefers short, affordable trips, having visited the historic city of Varanasi and the serene banks of the Ganga, while they dream of a weekend pilgrimage to Puri and a future road trip to the Himalayan foothills.
payload.culinary_persona: Subhash Chaudhary enjoys hearty meals of litti‑chokha, sattu paratha, and fish curry with mustard seeds, prefers the tangy flavor of raw mango pickle, and they often dine at the local dhaba where they also chat about art and logistics.
payload.persona: Subhash Chaudhary is a diligent logistics worker with a quiet love for Madhubani art, modest savings, and a regular habit of watching cricket, yet they occasionally splurge on a new TV set, reflecting their blend of practicality and quiet indulgence.
payload.cultural_background: Subhash belongs to the Maithil community of Supaul in rural Bihar. His daily life is shaped by the agrarian rhythms, close‑knit family ties, and the vibrant Mithila heritage that includes folk songs, seasonal festivals, and the famous Madhubani art form. He participates in village gatherings, observes local customs around marriage and harvest, and values the community’s emphasis on hospitality and collective support.
payload.linguistic_background: Subhash speaks Maithili as his mother tongue and is fluent in spoken Hindi, which he uses for daily transactions and communicating with traders. Being illiterate, he cannot read or write in any language, but he can recognize common symbols and signs, and he learns new words through listening to radio programmes and community storytelling.
payload.skills_and_expertise: With more than twenty years of experience as a loader and unloader, Subhash is adept at heavy lifting, arranging cargo for optimal weight distribution, and coordinating with truck drivers to ensure timely loading. He has practical knowledge of basic mechanical troubleshooting for equipment, understands local market schedules, and uses his curiosity for art to identify valuable items during transport. His reliability and organized approach make him a trusted worker in the supply chain.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Heavy lifting", "Cargo weight distribution", "Coordination with truck drivers", "Basic mechanical troubleshooting", "Local market schedule knowledge", "Madhubani art appreciation"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: In his free time, Subhash enjoys listening to Maithili folk songs, watching cricket matches on the village’s shared TV, and walking along the banks of the Kosi River. He has a keen eye for Madhubani patterns and often spends evenings discussing the designs with local artisans. He also likes gardening, growing seasonal vegetables, and reading aloud community newsletters circulated by the panchayat.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Maithili folk songs", "Watching cricket on village TV", "Walking by the Kosi River", "Discussing Madhubani designs with artisans", "Gardening seasonal vegetables", "Reading aloud community newsletters"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Subhash aspires to move from manual loading to a supervisory role in the local logistics hub, eventually overseeing a small team of loaders. He wishes to acquire basic literacy through an adult education program, enabling him to keep simple records and read work orders. In the longer term, he dreams of starting a modest transport service that can support his family and contribute to community development, while also promoting Mithila art by sponsoring local Madhubani workshops.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: Mr. Subhash Chaudhary, a 45-year-old male loader and unloader, is currently married and resides in rural Supaul, Bihar. He is illiterate, speaks Maithili, and has limited formal education, though he is fluent in spoken Hindi. His lifestyle includes a modest routine of daily walks, occasional viewing of cricket, and a quiet appreciation for Madhubani art.

Patient presented with a three-week history of productive cough, low-grade fever, and weight loss. Sputum sample was collected for microscopy and GeneXpert MTB/RIF assay. The patient has a history of hypertension and is on regular medication.

Results:
Sputum Microscopy (Ziehl-Neelsen stain): Acid-fast bacilli (AFB) seen in 2+ fields.
Sputum GeneXpert MTB/RIF: Detected Mycobacterium tuberculosis complex, Rifampicin resistance not detected.
Sputum Culture: Pending.
Complete Blood Count (CBC): Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL, Total Leukocyte Count 7,800 cells/µL.
Liver Function Test (LFT): SGOT 45 U/L, SGPT 52 U/L, Alkaline Phosphatase 78 U/L.
Renal Function Test (RFT): Serum Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, Blood Urea Nitrogen 18 mg/dL.
Blood Pressure: 145/90 mmHg.
Blood Glucose (Fasting): 110 mg/dL.

Interpretation:
The findings are consistent with Pulmonary Tuberculosis. The patient is also noted to have hypertension and mild anemia. He is advised to start anti-tubercular therapy as per the Revised National Tuberculosis Control Programme (RNTCP) guidelines.

Plan:
1. Initiate Four-Drug Regimen: Isoniazid, Rifampicin, Pyrazinamide, and Ethambutol.
2. Continue antihypertensive medication.
3. Provide nutritional counseling and support.
4. Schedule follow-up in two weeks for monitoring of treatment response and drug tolerance.
5. Contact [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sita Devi]] for DOTS support.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:

- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete ASHA Worker Note for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Maithili (code=mai, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Bihar, district=Supaul.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Maithili.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 45
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Loader and Unloader
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Bihar
- district: Supaul
- first_language: Maithili
- clinical_persona_summary: Mr. Subhash Chaudhary, a 45-year-old male loader and unloader, is married and resides in rural Supaul, Bihar. He is illiterate, speaks Maithili as his first language, and has limited formal education. His daily life is influenced by the agrarian rhythms and cultural heritage of the Maithil community. He has a modest, practical lifestyle with occasional discretionary spending.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|...]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|26]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|...]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Visit findings: <2-4 short sentences>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- RELIGION — Religion e.g. [[RELIGION|Hindu]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →

  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +

  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Hospital Billing / Invoice for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Malayalam (code=ml, script=Malayalam). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Kerala, district=Kannur.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Malayalam for Malayalam.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once (parking/ambulance or motor claim line). Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 45
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Market-Oriented Crop and Animal Producers, Other
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Rural
- state: Kerala
- district: Kannur
- first_language: Malayalam
- clinical_persona_summary: A 45-year-old male farmer from rural Kerala, currently married, with higher secondary education, who speaks Malayalam and has a competitive, stress-prone personality. He works in market-oriented crop and animal production, managing integrated farming systems while dealing with worry-driven stress. His lifestyle includes organizing local cricket matches and following Malayalam cinema.

Document format guide for Hospital Billing / Invoice (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Invoice — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] | Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | MRN [[MRN|...]]
Address district [[DISTRICT|...]] PIN [[PIN_CODE|110001]]
Ambulance / parking vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
Line items with amounts (no entity tags on procedure names).

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PIN_CODE — ZIP / Postal Code (PIN Code) e.g. [[PIN_CODE|208001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- AADHAAR_NUMBER — Aadhaar Number e.g. [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]
- INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER — Insurance Policy Number e.g. [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]
- TAX_ID — Tax Identification Number (TIN/GSTIN) e.g. [[TAX_ID|29ABCDE1234F1Z5]]
- TELEPHONE_LANDLINE — Telephone Number (Landline) e.g. [[TELEPHONE_LANDLINE|0512-2345678]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

payload.professional_persona: Anil Peter, a 45‑year‑old agronomist‑farmer in Kerala, combines deep expertise in market‑driven crop production, integrated pest management, soil health monitoring and drip irrigation with livestock, dairy and poultry operations, while spearheading farmer meetings, cooperative initiatives and competitively benchmarking yields for quality assurance.

payload.sports_persona: Anil Peter enjoys playing cricket with local youth, organizing evening matches at the village ground and coordinating friendly tournaments that feature teams like the Kannur Panthers and the Malabar Mavericks, showcasing their sociable and competitive spirit.
payload.arts_persona: Anil Peter is an avid fan of Malayalam cinema, regularly watching new releases starring Mohanlal and Mammootty, while also enjoying devotional gospel songs in Malayalam and attending cultural programmes featuring artists like K. S. Chithra and instrumentalist Shankar Mahadevan.
payload.travel_persona: Anil Peter prefers well‑planned road trips within Kerala, often visiting the backwaters of Alappuzha, the tea gardens of Munnar, and the historic forts of Kannur, while dreaming of a future family vacation to the serene beaches of the Andaman Islands, balancing budget constraints with a desire for cultural immersion.
payload.culinary_persona: Anil Peter enjoys cooking traditional Kerala dishes such as appam, puttu, and fish curry, relishing the aroma of fresh coconut, mustard seeds, and curry leaves, and prefers meals that blend tangy tamarind with the subtle heat of green chilies.
payload.persona: Anil Peter, a 45‑year‑old farmer‑leader, blends meticulous agronomic planning with a talkative, competitive edge while constantly wrestling with worry‑driven stress.
payload.cultural_background: Anil comes from the Malayali Christian community in the rural hinterland of Kannur, Kerala. His cultural life is rooted in the agrarian rhythms of the Malabar coast, the liturgical traditions of the local CSI church, and the vibrant customs of Kerala such as Onam celebrations adapted to his Christian family. He often takes a central role in community gatherings, reflecting his sociable and talkative nature, while his practical side keeps festivals well organised and predictable. The community values modesty, hospitality and strong family ties, and Anil’s competitive spirit pushes him to be a recognised local organiser.
payload.linguistic_background: Anil's mother tongue is Malayalam, which he speaks fluently and writes with complete literacy. His schooling up to Higher Secondary introduced him to English, enabling him to read newspapers, government notices, and market price bulletins, and to communicate functionally in English during trader negotiations and official visits. He does not have proficiency in any other Indian language.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Anil possesses deep expertise in market‑oriented crop production, integrated pest management, soil health monitoring, and drip irrigation. He is skilled in livestock breeding, dairy management, and poultry rearing. His business acumen includes market price analysis, trade negotiation, farm accounting, and navigating government subsidy schemes. He is also a strong community leader, comfortable in public speaking, organising farmer meetings, and coordinating cooperative activities. His competitive nature drives him to benchmark his yields against neighbouring farms, while his skeptical mindset ensures rigorous quality checks.

payload.hobbies_and_interests: Anil enjoys playing cricket with local youth in evening matches, often arranging friendly tournaments that let him showcase his sociable and talkative side. He is an avid fan of Malayalam cinema, keeping up with the latest releases. He loves singing in the church choir and listening to devotional music, especially gospel songs in Malayalam. Cooking traditional Kerala dishes such as appam, puttu and fish curry is a favorite pastime. He reads articles on financial planning and periodically attends stress‑management workshops to cope with his worry‑prone nature.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Cricket", "Malayalam Cinema", "Church Choir Singing", "Cooking Kerala Cuisine", "Reading Financial Planning Articles", "Attending Stress Management Workshops"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Anil aims to expand his farm to 5 hectares in the next five years, focusing on high‑value spices and organic vegetables. payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-0912]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Kannur District Hospital]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anil Menon]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Kunjikannan]] [[AGE|45]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|Kannur]]
Reason: Persistent cough with weight loss and night sweats, requiring comprehensive evaluation for pulmonary tuberculosis and assessment of co-morbid non-communicable diseases.

Dear Sir/Madam,

We are referring [[PATIENT_NAME|Kunjikannan]], a 45-year-old male farmer from rural Kannur, for further investigation and management. The patient presents with a three-month history of productive cough, intermittent low-grade fever, and significant weight loss of approximately 8 kg. He also reports night sweats and mild dyspnea on exertion. His past medical history is significant for hypertension, for which he is on irregular medication.

On examination, the patient is cachectic with a BMI of 17.5 kg/m2. His blood pressure is 150/95 mmHg, and his pulse is 98 beats per minute. Chest auscultation reveals diminished breath sounds in the right upper zone with occasional crepitations. A provisional diagnosis of pulmonary tuberculosis with probable associated hypertension is made.

Investigations at our facility include a sputum sample for GeneXpert MTB/RIF testing, which is pending. A chest X-ray shows a right upper lobe infiltrate with cavitation. Baseline blood investigations reveal a random blood glucose of 140 mg/dL and an HbA1c of 7.2%.

We request you to please evaluate the patient for anti-tubercular therapy and manage his hypertension and potential diabetes mellitus. Please provide your expert opinion and treatment plan at the earliest.

For any further information, please contact us at [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]. The patient's father, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Kunjiraman]], can be reached at the same number.

Yours sincerely,
[[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anil Menon]]
[[HOSPITAL_NAME|Kannur District Hospital]]
[[MRN|MRN-KNR-2024-0815-001]]
[[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P1234567]]
[[DRIVING_LICENCE|KL04 20100012345]]

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:

GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:

GOOD:

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
