BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Manipuri (code=mni, script=Meitei). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Manipur, district=Thoubal.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Meitei for Manipuri.

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 21
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Sports Goods Maker, Wood
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Manipur
- district: Thoubal
- first_language: Manipuri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 21-year-old female from rural Thoubal, Manipur, with primary education, who works as a woodcraft maker. She is never married, speaks Manipuri as her first language, and follows a routine-focused lifestyle with attention to detail in her craft. Her background includes a small agrarian village, limited formal schooling, and a community-oriented life centered on traditional wooden craft and local festivals.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton — put demographics + device/network IDs ONLY in a short
session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|...]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
--- chat ---
Patient: <symptom turn — no AGE/GENDER/PHONE re-tags>
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]: <advice turn>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
- URL — URL (Personal Website/Profile) e.g. [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
- IMEI_NUMBER — IMEI Number e.g. [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Phanjom Chanu]] [[AGE|21]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|phan.chanu@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Thoubal District Hospital]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Namaste doctor, I am Phanjom Chanu from Thoubal. I have a sharp pain in my right lower abdomen for the past two days. It started after I was carving wood for a long time. The pain is constant and sometimes it becomes very severe. I also feel a bit feverish.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Sharma]]: Hello Phanjom, thank you for reaching out. I understand you are having pain in your right lower abdomen. Can you tell me if the pain is sharp or dull? Is it getting worse when you move or press the area?
Patient: It is very sharp, doctor. It hurts a lot when I walk or bend. I have not been able to do my woodcraft work at all.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Sharma]]: I see. Based on your description of sharp, localized pain in the right lower abdomen with fever, it sounds like you might have acute appendicitis. This is a condition where the appendix, a small pouch attached to your large intestine, becomes inflamed.
Patient: Appendicitis? Is it serious? Do I need to go to the hospital?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Sharma]]: Yes, it can be serious and requires prompt medical attention. I strongly recommend you go to the Thoubal District Hospital immediately. They will likely need to do a physical examination and some blood tests to confirm the diagnosis. In most cases, surgery is needed to remove the appendix.
Patient: Okay, doctor. I will go to the hospital right away. What should I do before I go?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Sharma]]: Please do not eat or drink anything, as you may need surgery. You can take a paracetamol tablet if the fever is bothering you, but avoid any other painkillers. It is very important that you get to the hospital as soon as possible. Do you have someone who can take you?
Patient: Yes, my cousin is with me. We are leaving now. Thank you, doctor.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Sharma]]: You are welcome, Phanjom. Please go to the emergency department at the hospital. I will inform them about your case. Take care and get well soon.

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Surgical / Operative Note for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Manipuri (code=mni, script=Meitei). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Manipur, district=Thoubal.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Meitei for Manipuri.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 21
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Sports Goods Maker, Wood
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Manipur
- district: Thoubal
- first_language: Manipuri
- clinical_persona_summary: A 21-year-old female from rural Thoubal, Manipur, with primary education, works as a woodcraft maker. She is never married and speaks Manipuri as her first language. Her lifestyle includes a focus on precise measurements and a quiet routine, with limited proficiency in Hindi.

Document format guide for Surgical / Operative Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|50]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Procedure / findings: MUST describe an actual procedure/operation matching the
assigned clinical domain (not a psychiatry admission note). Keep relative names
gender-plausible.

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Thoubal District Hospital]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-1025]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Leima Devi]] [[AGE|21]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Khuman Singh]]
Procedure / findings: Patient is a 21-year-old female from rural Thoubal, Manipur, who presented with a traumatic laceration to the left forearm sustained while working with woodcraft materials. Initial examination revealed a 4 cm deep, irregular laceration with moderate bleeding and contamination from sawdust. The patient was hemodynamically stable with vitals BP 110/70 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, SpO2 98% on room air. The wound was thoroughly irrigated with normal saline and local anesthesia with 2% lignocaine was administered. Under aseptic conditions, the wound was debrided of all foreign material and devitalized tissue. The laceration was then meticulously repaired in layers using 3-0 absorbable sutures for deep dermal approximation and 4-0 nylon for skin closure. A sterile dressing was applied. The procedure was completed in approximately 45 minutes without any intraoperative complications. The patient tolerated the procedure well and was transferred to the post-operative recovery ward for observation.
Post-op plan: Continue observation for 24 hours. Monitor for signs of infection. Administer oral antibiotics (Amoxicillin 500 mg TID for 5 days) and analgesics (Paracetamol 500 mg QID PRN). Wound care instructions provided. Follow-up in 3 days.
[[BED_NUMBER|15]]
[[MRN|MRN-2024-1025-001]]
[[PATIENT_ID|PID-THB-2024-001]]
[[RELATIVE_NAME|Nongmaithem Sanatombi]]

payload.professional_persona: Lilati Devi is a 21‑year‑old woodcraft artisan who designs and hand‑makes cricket bats, hockey sticks and traditional game implements, applying precise measuring, ergonomic design and diligent quality checks while balancing her love for routine with a relaxed, sometimes chaotic, approach to business planning.
payload.linguistic_persona: Lilati Devi speaks Manipuri as her mother tongue, can read and write simple sentences in the Meitei Mayek script, has limited conversational Hindi and English skills, and comfortably follows workshop instructions in Manipuri while using Hindi numerals for basic measurements.
payload.sports_persona: Lilati Devi enjoys informal village cricket matches using her own handcrafted bats, avidly follows the Manipur state cricket team and the Manipur Women's Hockey squad, occasionally joins a local community jogging group for early‑morning runs, and stays fit by practicing basic field drills at the village ground.
payload.arts_persona: Lilati Devi delights in listening to traditional Pena performances by maestro Kanhailal, reads Manipuri folk stories collected by Mairengbam, enjoys Bollywood films starring Deepika Padukone for a break, and occasionally attends local cultural events featuring Manipuri dance troupes, finding inspiration for her wood carvings.
payload.travel_persona: Lilati Devi has explored nearby Imphal markets and the scenic Loktak Lake on weekend trips, dreams of backpacking to Shillong and Darjeeling to photograph Himalayan landscapes, plans her travels modestly due to limited workshop income, and prefers organized group tours with friends over solitary adventures.
payload.culinary_persona: Lilati Devi relishes tangy Eromba made with fermented fish (ngari) and mustard leaves, crisp Singju salad with lotus stems, and sweet Cham Cham dessert, usually eats at the village dhaba with occasional help from her mother in the kitchen, and enjoys sipping traditional rice beer (chahong) during festivals.
payload.persona: Lilati Devi is a diligent woodcraft prodigy who thrives on quiet routines, frets over every measurement, and they secretly hoard spare nails and twine for comfort.
payload.cultural_background: Lilati Devi hails from a small agrarian village in Thoubal district, Manipur. She belongs to the Meitei Hindu community where daily life revolves around farming cycles, village festivals and a long-standing tradition of wooden craft. The local culture emphasizes close-knit families, mutual help and the practical ingenuity (jugaad) needed to make sports gear from locally sourced timber. Despite the communal atmosphere, she prefers the quiet of her own workshop where structure and predictability dominate her routine.
payload.linguistic_background: Lilati’s mother tongue is Manipuri (Meitei). Thanks to primary schooling she can read and write simple sentences in the Manipuri script (now often written in the Indigenous Meitei Mayek or Bengali script). She has limited proficiency in Hindi and English, relying mainly on oral communication in her village. At the workshop she follows instructions written in Manipuri, and occasionally uses Hindi numerals for basic measurements.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Lilati has honed practical woodworking skills focused on crafting wooden sports equipment such as cricket bats, hockey sticks, and traditional Manipuri game implements. She is proficient in measuring, cutting, carving, sanding and finishing timber using basic hand tools. Her attention to ergonomic design ensures that the equipment is lightweight yet durable. She also manages small-scale inventory, maintains quality checks, and interacts with local customers, applying simple bookkeeping for her modest workshop.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Wooden sports equipment fabrication', 'Carving and shaping wood', 'Tool handling (saws, chisels, sandpaper)', 'Ergonomic design', 'Quality inspection', 'Small-scale inventory management', 'Customer interaction', 'Basic bookkeeping', 'Problem solving with limited resources (jugaad)', 'Understanding of local sports equipment standards']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Lilati enjoys quiet pastimes such as reading Manipuri folk stories, listening to traditional Pena music, walking along the village fields, and carving small decorative wooden pieces for personal satisfaction. She occasionally joins a few friends for informal village cricket matches and takes pleasure in collecting wildflowers during her walks. Crafting and nature provide her an outlet to unwind from daily stress.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Reading Manipuri folk stories', 'Listening to traditional Pena music', 'Walking in village fields', 'Carving small decorative wooden pieces', 'Playing informal village cricket matches', 'Collecting wildflowers']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Lilati aspires to transform her modest workshop into a small-scale enterprise that supplies quality wooden sports gear to schools and local clubs across Manipur. She plans a step-by-step roadmap, balancing structured milestones with room for improvisation. She aims to acquire formal training in carpentry and product design, purchase a power saw to improve efficiency, and develop a basic bookkeeping system to track income. Her long-term ambition is to create steady employment for a few village youths and achieve financial stability that eases her worries about unpredictable earnings.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 21-year-old female from rural Thoubal, Manipur, with primary education, who works as a woodcraft maker. She is never married and speaks Manipuri as her first language. Her lifestyle is characterized by a focus on precise, routine-based work and a quiet, diligent nature.

- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).

Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:

GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:

  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.

Write one complete Lab Report for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Sangli.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Marathi.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 54
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Rural
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Sangli
- first_language: Marathi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 54-year-old female, currently married, retired homemaker with a matriculation education, resides in a rural area of Sangli, Maharashtra, and speaks Marathi as her first language. She is a community cultural enthusiast who engages in traditional arts and local customs, with modest English proficiency. Her lifestyle includes participation in community arts and occasional walking, reflecting an active, socially integrated routine.

Document format guide for Lab Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (English pivot; TYPE names stay ASCII forever):
Lab report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Barddhaman District Hospital]] District [[DISTRICT|Purba Bardhaman]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-WB-2024-0815]] Patient ID [[PATIENT_ID|PID-WB-7781]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] [[AGE|27]] [[GENDER|Female]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Ordered by [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Riya Banerjee]]
Results table (analyte values NOT tagged as PHI entities).
NEVER write [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] / [[এম.আর.এন|…]] — only PATIENT_NAME, MRN, etc.

MANDATORY inline entities (9 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.

payload.generated_text:
Lab report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sangli District Hospital]] District [[DISTRICT|Sangli]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-MH-2024-0815]] Patient ID [[PATIENT_ID|PID-MH-7781]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Savitri Patil]] [[AGE|54]] [[GENDER|Female]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Ordered by [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Deshmukh]]
Results table (analyte values NOT tagged as PHI entities).
PATIENT NAME: Savitri Patil
AGE: 54 years
GENDER: Female
HOSPITAL NAME: Sangli District Hospital
MRN: MRN-MH-2024-0815
DOCTOR NAME: Dr. Rajesh Deshmukh
DISTRICT: Sangli
PHONE NUMBER: 9876543210
PATIENT ID: PID-MH-7781

LABORATORY FINDINGS
Date of collection: 15 August 2024
Sample type: Blood
Test panel: Complete Blood Count with Differential, Comprehensive Metabolic Panel, Thyroid Function Tests, Vitamin D (25-OH), Vitamin B12, Folate, Serum Cortisol (morning), Urine Drug Screen.

COMPLETE BLOOD COUNT
Hemoglobin: 12.8 g/dL (Normal: 12.0-15.5 g/dL)
White Blood Cell Count: 7.2 x 10^9/L (Normal: 4.5-11.0 x 10^9/L)
Neutrophils: 55% (Normal: 40-74%)
Lymphocytes: 30% (Normal: 20-45%)
Monocytes: 10% (Normal: 2-8%)
Eosinophils: 4% (Normal: 0-6%)
Platelets: 215 x 10^9/L (Normal: 150-450 x 10^9/L)

COMPREHENSIVE METABOLIC PANEL
Sodium: 138 mEq/L (Normal: 135-145 mEq/L)
Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L (Normal: 3.5-5.1 mEq/L)
Chloride: 101 mEq/L (Normal: 98-106 mEq/L)
Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L (Normal: 22-28 mEq/L)
Blood Urea Nitrogen (BUN): 15 mg/dL (Normal: 7-20 mg/dL)
Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL (Normal: 0.6-1.1 mg/dL)
Glucose: 92 mg/dL (Normal: 70-100 mg/dL)
Calcium: 9.2 mg/dL (Normal: 8.5-10.5 mg/dL)
Total Protein: 7.1 g/dL (Normal: 6.4-8.3 g/dL)
Albumin: 4.0 g/dL (Normal: 3.5-5.0 g/dL)
Total Bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL (Normal: 0.3-1.0 mg/dL)
Direct Bilirubin: 0.2 mg/dL (Normal: 0.0-0.3 mg/dL)
Alkaline Phosphatase (ALP): 65 U/L (Normal: 40-129 U/L)
Alanine Aminotransferase (ALT): 22 U/L (Normal: 7-55 U/L)
Aspartate Aminotransferase (AST): 25 U/L (Normal: 8-48 U/L)
Lipase: 35 U/L (Normal: 10-140 U/L)

THYROID FUNCTION TESTS
Thyroid Stimulating Hormone (TSH): 2.5 mIU/L (Normal: 0.4-4.0 mIU/L)
Free Thyroxine (T4): 1.2 ng/dL (Normal: 0.8-1.8 ng/dL)
Free Triiodothyronine (T3): 3.8 pg/mL (Normal: 2.3-4.2 pg/mL)

VITAMIN LEVELS
Vitamin D (25-OH): 18 ng/mL (Normal: 30-100 ng/mL) - Low
Vitamin B12: 450 pg/mL (Normal: 200-900 pg/mL) - Low
Folate: 12 ng/mL (Normal: 5.4-19.2 ng/mL) - Low

HORMONAL ASSESSMENT
Morning Serum Cortisol: 14.5 mcg/dL (Normal: 6.0-23.0 mcg/dL)

URINE DRUG SCREEN
Result: Negative for Amphetamines, Cocaine, Marijuana, Opiates, PCP, and Benzodiazepines.

CLINICAL IMPRESSION
The laboratory findings reveal mild normocytic anemia, likely secondary to nutritional deficiencies. Vitamin D, B12, and Folate levels are low, which may contribute to fatigue and low mood. Thyroid function is within normal limits. The urine drug screen is negative. These results support a diagnosis of depressive disorder with somatic features and nutritional deficiencies.

RECOMMENDATIONS
1. Initiate supplementation with Vitamin D 2000 IU daily, Vitamin B12 1000 mcg intramuscularly weekly for 4 weeks, followed by oral maintenance, and Folic Acid 1 mg daily.
2. Re-evaluate complete blood count and vitamin levels in 8 weeks.
3. Continue psychotherapy and consider initiation of an SSRI antidepressant after discussing risks and benefits with the patient.
4. Encourage dietary modifications and safe sun exposure for Vitamin D synthesis.
5. Follow-up with Dr. Rajesh Deshmukh in 2 weeks to review symptoms and medication tolerance.

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete ASHA Worker Note for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Marathi (code=mr, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Maharashtra, district=Sangli.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Marathi.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 54
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Rural
- state: Maharashtra
- district: Sangli
- first_language: Marathi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 54-year-old female from Maharashtra’s Sangli district, she is married and retired with a matriculation education, living in a rural setting. Her first language is Marathi, with functional Hindi and limited English. She maintains an active lifestyle through community walking and local sports.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|...]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|26]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|...]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Visit findings: <2-4 short sentences>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- RELIGION — Religion e.g. [[RELIGION|Hindu]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.

payload.professional_persona: Rajesh Kumar is a 43‑year‑old seasoned plasterer in the hilly terrains of Shimla, skillfully blending modern cement mixes with traditional lime techniques, and they lead small crews with a practical ‘jugaad’ mindset while planning to formalise their trade through a certified contracting unit.
payload.linguistic_persona: Rajesh Kumar grew up speaking Nepali at home and uses it for storytelling, while they have functional Hindi skills for market and school interactions, despite being formally illiterate and relying on their wife or younger relatives for reading and writing tasks.
payload.sports_persona: Rajesh Kumar enjoys watching cricket matches on TV, cheering for the Himachal Pradesh cricket team, and maintains fitness through weekly trekking in the hills and occasional neighborhood badminton games, while they are a regular member of the local cricket fan club.
payload.arts_persona: Rajesh Kumar savours folk ballads sung by local hill singers and listens to regional melodies by artists such as Kailash Kher, often humming them while they carve simple wooden figurines for décor, reflecting a deep appreciation for regional music and craftsmanship.
payload.travel_persona: Rajesh Kumar enjoys weekend trekking in the Shimla hills, occasional trips to nearby Dharamshala for cultural festivals, and dreams of exploring Ladakh's stark desert landscapes, balancing modest travel budgets with a love for mountain scenery.
payload.culinary_persona: Rajesh Kumar delights in preparing and sharing traditional dishes such as dal‑bhat, gundruk, regional siddu and madra, and they enjoy savoring local street food like aloo tikki chaat, often adding fresh herbs from their garden to enhance flavours.
payload.persona: Rajesh Kumar is a pragmatic plasterer who blends traditional craftsmanship with modern techniques, they cherish mountain folk culture, and they balance modest ambition with a love for community, occasional indulgence, and a resilient, curiosity‑driven spirit.
payload.cultural_background: Rajesh is a member of the Nepali‑speaking Gorkha community in the rural hills of Shimla, Himachal Pradesh. His upbringing blends Nepali cultural practices—such as communal gatherings, love for folk songs, and respect for elders—with Himachali customs like local festivals, traditional dress and the practical, self‑reliant spirit of mountain villages. As a Hindu, his cultural life is anchored in rituals and festivals that draw from both Nepali and Himachali traditions, allowing him to navigate a multicultural identity while staying grounded in his rural roots.
payload.linguistic_background: Rajesh grew up speaking Nepali at home and in the local Gorkha circles, which remains his most comfortable language for daily conversation and storytelling. Hindi is his second language, learnt through schoolyard interactions, market dealings and television, enabling him to communicate with the broader Shimla community and customers. Although he is formally illiterate, he possesses functional language skills: he can understand spoken instructions, recognise and count basic numbers, read familiar signs like Plaster or Cement in Devanagari, and rely on his wife or younger relatives for any required reading or writing. His linguistic ability reflects a practical curiosity, balancing oral tradition with the necessities of his trade.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Rajesh has over two decades of hands‑on experience as a plasterer in the hilly terrain of Himachal. He is skilled at mixing cement, lime and sand in the correct proportions, applying smooth coats to both new and old walls, and repairing cracks or weak sections using both modern compounds and traditional lime‑based techniques. His practical know‑how includes estimating material quantities, using hand tools such as trowel, hawk and spray, and coordinating small crews to complete jobs on schedule. He also carries a local «jugaad» mindset, improvising solutions when resources are limited, and is knowledgeable about traditional Himachali construction methods that blend stone, wood and mud.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Plastering', 'Mixing cement, lime and sand', 'Surface smoothing and finishing', 'Repairing cracks and weak walls', 'Estimating material quantities', 'Using hand tools (trowel, hawk, spray)', 'Coordinating small work crews', 'Knowledge of traditional Himachali building techniques']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Rajesh enjoys trekking in the nearby hills, listening to Nepali and Himachali folk music, playing cards with neighbours, watching cricket matches on television, preparing traditional Nepali and Himachali dishes at home, attending local festivals and fairs, gardening and growing his own vegetables, and occasionally doing simple woodcarving as a relaxing hobby.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Trekking in the hills', 'Listening to folk music', 'Playing cards with neighbours', 'Watching cricket on television', 'Cooking traditional Nepali and Himachali dishes', 'Attending local festivals and fairs', 'Gardening and growing vegetables', 'Simple wood carving']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Rajesh aspires to formalise his plastering trade by registering a small contracting unit that can take on larger projects in the Shimla district. He plans to enrol in government‑run skill‑development programmes to earn a recognised certification, which will help him command better rates and manage paperwork more efficiently. With a modest goal of earning a few lakhs per annum, he wishes to provide a comfortable education for his children, save for his family’s health needs and eventually purchase a modest house in a better‑connected part of the town. He also hopes to pass on his practical knowledge to his sons, incorporating eco‑friendly lime‑plaster techniques and a modern safety mindset while retaining the traditional “jugaad” spirit.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 43-year-old male plasterer from rural Himachal Pradesh, currently married, with an illiterate education level. He speaks Nepali as his first language and works in the construction industry, blending traditional techniques with modern methods. His lifestyle includes occasional trekking and a preference for local folk culture.

- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.

payload.generated_text:
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Indira Gandhi Medical College and Hospital]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|IGMC-SHM-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Ramesh Singh]] | DOB [[DOB|1981-07-22]] | Age: [[AGE|43]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Male]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Plasterer]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-0815-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sunita Devi]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-8834]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Shimla]]
Chief complaint: Patient presents with a two-week history of persistent dry cough and low-grade fever, associated with occasional chest discomfort and generalized body aches.

History of present illness: The patient, a 43-year-old male plasterer from a rural area of Shimla district, reports that the cough started insidiously and has been progressively worsening. He describes the cough as non-productive and worse in the evenings. He has experienced low-grade fever, measured at home as around 99.5°F, for the past ten days. He also complains of a dull, aching pain in the central chest, which he rates as 4/10 in severity. He attributes his symptoms to exposure to dust at his workplace. He denies any shortness of breath, wheezing, or hemoptysis. He has been taking over-the-counter analgesics and cough suppressants with minimal relief.

Past medical history: No known chronic illnesses. No history of tuberculosis or any known contact with TB patients. He has never undergone any major surgeries.

Personal history: He is a non-smoker and does not consume alcohol or any tobacco products. He works as a plasterer, which involves significant exposure to construction dust. He lives in a joint family setup.

Examination:
General: The patient is a well-built, moderately built male, appearing mildly fatigued. He is conscious and oriented.
Vital signs: Temperature: 99.2°F, Pulse: 88/min, Respiratory rate: 18/min, Blood pressure: 120/80 mmHg, SpO2: 97% on room air.
Systemic examination:
Respiratory: Chest expansion is symmetrical. Percussion note is dull in the right infra-axillary region. Breath sounds are vesicular with diminished intensity in the right lower zone. No added sounds heard.
Cardiovascular: S1 and S2 are audible. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Other systems: Examination of other systems is unremarkable.

Investigations:
Chest X-ray (PA view): Shows a subtle opacity in the right lower lobe.
Complete blood count (CBC): Hemoglobin: 14.5 g/dL, Total WBC count: 9,800/cmm with a neutrophilic predominance.
Sputum for routine examination and culture: Sent for analysis.

Diagnosis:
1. Acute lower respiratory tract infection, right lower lobe pneumonia.
2. Occupational lung irritation.

Treatment and Plan:
1. Started on a course of oral Azithromycin 500 mg once daily for 3 days.
2. Prescribed Paracetamol 500 mg for fever and body ache as needed.
3. Advised to take adequate rest and increase fluid intake.
4. Counselled on the importance of using a proper mask (N95) at the workplace to prevent dust inhalation.
5. Follow-up appointment scheduled in one week to review symptoms and investigate reports.
6. Patient advised to return immediately if he develops shortness of breath or high-grade fever.

Follow-up: [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-2024-0822-001]]

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|50]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Procedure / findings: MUST describe an actual procedure/operation matching the
assigned clinical domain (not a psychiatry admission note). Keep relative names
gender-plausible.

- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]

- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.

payload.generated_text:
Operative note — District Hospital Shimla Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-1123]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Krishna Bahadur Thapa]] [[AGE|43]] [[GENDER|Male]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]
Procedure / findings: The patient is a 43-year-old male plasterer from Shimla, Himachal Pradesh, admitted with a history of chronic cough and weight loss. Investigations revealed a cavitary lesion in the right upper lobe, and sputum for AFB was positive. The patient was started on DOTS therapy. On day 15 of admission, the patient developed hemoptysis and a chest X-ray showed an enlarging cavity with a thick wall. A decision was made for percutaneous CT-guided aspiration of the cavity. The procedure was performed under local anesthesia with the patient in the prone position. A 22-gauge needle was advanced into the cavity under CT guidance. Aspiration yielded thick, purulent material which was sent for culture and sensitivity. The cavity was irrigated with 10 ml of normal saline. The patient tolerated the procedure well. Post-procedure, the patient was observed in the recovery room for 2 hours and then shifted back to the ward. The patient was advised to continue with anti-tubercular therapy and follow-up with the chest physician.
Discharge summary: The patient was discharged on day 20 of admission with a prescription for anti-tubercular therapy. The patient was advised to follow up with the chest physician after 2 weeks. The patient's relative, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Bahadur Thapa]], was counseled on the importance of completing the full course of treatment. The patient's [[MRN|MRN-2024-1123-001]] and [[PATIENT_ID|PID-SHM-4301]] have been updated in the system. The patient was assigned [[BED_NUMBER|25]] in [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]] ward. The patient's [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Shimla]] [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-1123]] details are recorded.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:

- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Prescription for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Nepali (code=ne, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Himachal Pradesh, district=Shimla.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Nepali.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 43
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Plasterer
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Himachal Pradesh
- district: Shimla
- first_language: Nepali
- clinical_persona_summary: A 43-year-old male plasterer from rural Himachal Pradesh, currently married, with an illiterate education level, who speaks Nepali as his first language. He works in the construction sector and resides in the Shimla district. His lifestyle includes occasional alcohol consumption and a preference for community-oriented activities.

Document format guide for Prescription (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Prescription — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Male]], MRN [[MRN|RX-1001]]
Dr. [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Rx: <drug dose frequency>  (do NOT tag drug names as entities)

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS — Residential Address e.g. [[RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS|12 Gandhi Nagar, Ward 4]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

payload.professional_persona: An experienced retired homemaker and community elder, Gulapi Gopal coordinates daily puja, weaves simple hand‑loom fabrics, mentors younger women in safeguarding traditional Odia cooking and craft techniques, and they channel their vivid imagination into lively storytelling and spontaneous bhajan performances.
payload.linguistic_persona: A fluent Odia speaker, Gulapi Gopal flawlessly recites verses, recipes and folk songs from memory, despite being illiterate in the Odia script and possessing only rudimentary Hindi from occasional market encounters, and they rely on oral tradition for all communication.
payload.sports_persona: An enthusiastic cricket fan and supporter of Odisha’s Ranji team, Gulapi Gopal gathers with neighbours to cheer on matches, enjoys daily morning walks through the paddy fields, and they occasionally join light‑hearted kabaddi games at village fairs.
payload.arts_persona: A fervent lover of Odissi music and dance, Gulapi Gopal sings Akshaya Mohanty’s bhajans with heartfelt devotion, treasures traditional folk ballads of Babulal Bhoi, admires legendary dancer Kelucharan Mohapatra, and eagerly watches Sudarsan Pattnaik’s sand‑art installations whenever they visit Puri’s coastline.
payload.travel_persona: A spirited pilgrim, Gulapi Gopal frequently visits Puri’s Jagannath Temple, takes short trips to the Konark Sun Temple, enjoys occasional weekend getaways to Kolkata for Durga Puja celebrations, and they dream of a tranquil summer stay in the hills of Darjeeling.
payload.culinary_persona: A culinary virtuoso of traditional Odia fare, Gulapi Gopal expertly crafts pakhala bhata, dalma, chakuli pitha, and sugary rasgulla, and they season them with fresh coconut, mustard oil, turmeric and a pinch of asafoetida to delight family gatherings.
payload.persona: Gulapi Gopal’s colorful imagination turns routine chores into lively performances, yet their habit of misplacing medication and forgetting appointments makes them endearingly scattered.
payload.cultural_background: Gulapi Gopal grew up in a traditional Odia household in the agrarian villages surrounding Khordha, Odisha. The rural setting is characterised by paddy fields, coconut groves and a village temple that serves as the social and religious hub. Her upbringing was infused with Odia customs such as the celebration of Raja, Durga Puja and the harvest festival Pana Sankranti, as well as the oral transmission of folk tales, dance, and music. The community’s emphasis on collective harmony and respect for elders shaped her worldview, while the region’s rich tradition of hand-loom weaving, temple art and ritual cuisine provided the canvas for her imaginative and artistic leanings.
payload.linguistic_background: Her mother tongue is Odia, which she uses fluently for all daily interactions, from household chores to community gatherings. Due to lifelong illiteracy, she cannot read or write in Odia script, but she possesses an excellent oral command, remembering verses, recipes and folk songs by heart. She has limited exposure to Hindi, mainly through occasional radio broadcasts and visits to the nearby market, but she does not speak it confidently. Her lack of formal schooling means she relies on visual cues and memory rather than written instruction.
payload.skills_and_expertise: She possesses practical household skills honed over decades of managing a large rural home. These include preparing a variety of traditional Odia cuisines, weaving simple hand-loom textiles, maintaining a kitchen garden, conducting family puja and religious ceremonies, storytelling through folk tales, and basic arithmetic for market transactions. Her artistic instinct also finds expression in rangoli design, bamboo craft, and improvisational singing during festivals.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Traditional Odia cooking", "Hand-loom weaving", "Kitchen gardening", "Conducting family puja and rituals", "Storytelling (folk tales)", "Basic arithmetic for market transactions", "Rangoli design", "Bamboo craft", "Improvisational singing during festivals", "Organising community celebrations"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: She delights in singing devotional Odissi bhajans, attending village fairs and Rath Yatra, creating colorful rangoli patterns, tending to her small garden of seasonal greens, listening to folk music on the radio, playing group games like Antakshari with neighbours, memorising and reciting classic Odia poetry, crafting simple jewellery from shells and beads, and engaging in storytelling sessions with her grandchildren.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Singing devotional Odissi bhajans", "Attending village fairs and Rath Yatra", "Creating colorful rangoli designs", "Tending a seasonal kitchen garden", "Listening to folk music on the radio", "Playing group games like Antakshari with neighbours", "Memorising and reciting classic Odia poetry", "Crafting simple jewellery from shells and beads", "Storytelling sessions with grandchildren"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: As a septuagenarian retired homemaker, she aspires to preserve and transmit her rich repository of Odia culinary, craft and folk knowledge to younger generations, especially her grandchildren. She aims to mentor local women in traditional cooking and weaving, volunteer in the village temple committee, and document oral stories so they are not lost. Maintaining good health, staying socially active, and contributing to community welfare are also central to her ambitions.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 79-year-old female, widowed, retired homemaker from a rural Odisha district, with an illiterate education level and Odia as her first language. She presents with scattered cognition, evidenced by medication misplacement and appointment forgetfulness, but maintains strong oral communication skills. Clinically, she is likely to have limited health literacy and may require simplified instructions for management.

payload.user_prompt:

ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Khordha.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Odia for Odia.

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 79
- marital_status: Widowed
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Odisha
- district: Khordha
- first_language: Odia
- clinical_persona_summary: A 79-year-old female, widowed, retired homemaker from a rural Odisha district, with an illiterate education level and Odia as her first language. She presents with scattered cognition, evidenced by medication misplacement and appointment forgetfulness, but maintains strong oral communication skills. Clinically, she is likely to have limited health literacy and may require simplified instructions for management.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton — put demographics + device/network IDs ONLY in a short
session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|...]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
--- chat ---
Patient: <symptom turn — no AGE/GENDER/PHONE re-tags>
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]: <advice turn>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
- URL — URL (Personal Website/Profile) e.g. [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
- IMEI_NUMBER — IMEI Number e.g. [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Sushila Devi]] [[AGE|79]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240815-01]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|sushila.devi@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Khordha]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Namaste doctor. I am Sushila Devi from Khordha. I have been feeling very weak and tired for the last few days. I also have a mild fever and my body is aching all over. I forgot to take my BP medicine this morning.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Patnaik]]: Namaste Sushila Devi. I understand you are feeling weak, tired, with fever and body aches. This is concerning. Have you been taking your blood pressure medication regularly?
Patient: Sometimes I forget, doctor. My memory is not so good these days. I live alone. My son is in Cuttack. I also have a cough now.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Patnaik]]: It is important to take your BP medicine on time. Forgetting can be dangerous. The cough and fever suggest you might have an infection. I am prescribing some basic tests and medicines. Please go to the District Hospital Khordha tomorrow morning.
Patient: Okay doctor. What tests should I do? I am worried about the cost.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Patnaik]]: Do not worry about the cost. Get a complete blood count (CBC) and a blood sugar test done. The hospital will guide you. I am also sending a prescription for fever and cough medicine to your registered mobile number.
Patient: Thank you, doctor. I will go to the hospital tomorrow. What about my BP medicine?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Patnaik]]: For today, please take one tablet of Amlodipine 5mg which I am sending in the prescription. From tomorrow, please ensure you take your BP medicine every morning at the same time. I will also arrange for a local ASHA worker to visit you at home.
Patient: That is very kind of you, doctor. I will take the medicine and go for the tests. Thank you.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rina Patnaik]]: You are welcome, Sushila Devi. Please take care of yourself. If you feel very unwell, you can call me again. Take the medicines as prescribed.

payload.professional_persona: An experienced retired homemaker and community elder, Gulapi Gopal coordinates daily puja, weaves simple hand‑loom fabrics, mentors younger women in safeguarding traditional Odia cooking and craft techniques, and they channel their vivid imagination into lively storytelling and spontaneous bhajan performances.
payload.linguistic_persona: A fluent Odia speaker, Gulapi Gopal flawlessly recites verses, recipes and folk songs from memory, despite being illiterate in the Odia script and possessing only rudimentary Hindi from occasional market encounters, and they rely on oral tradition for all communication.
payload.sports_persona: An enthusiastic cricket fan and supporter of Odisha’s Ranji team, Gulapi Gopal gathers with neighbours to cheer on matches, enjoys daily morning walks through the paddy fields, and they occasionally join light‑hearted kabaddi games at village fairs.
payload.arts_persona: A fervent lover of Odissi music and dance, Gulapi Gopal sings Akshaya Mohanty’s bhajans with heartfelt devotion, treasures traditional folk ballads of Babulal Bhoi, admires legendary dancer Kelucharan Mohapatra, and eagerly watches Sudarsan Pattnaik’s sand‑art installations whenever they visit Puri’s coastline.
payload.travel_persona: A spirited pilgrim, Gulapi Gopal frequently visits Puri’s Jagannath Temple, takes short trips to the Konark Sun Temple, enjoys occasional weekend getaways to Kolkata for Durga Puja celebrations, and they dream of a tranquil summer stay in the hills of Darjeeling.
payload.culinary_persona: A culinary virtuoso of traditional Odia fare, Gulapi Gopal expertly crafts pakhala bhata, dalma, chakuli pitha, and sugary rasgulla, and they season them with fresh coconut, mustard oil, turmeric and a pinch of asafoetida to delight family gatherings.
payload.persona: Gulapi Gopal’s colorful imagination turns routine chores into lively performances, yet their habit of misplacing medication and forgetting appointments makes them endearingly scattered.
payload.cultural_background: Gulapi Gopal grew up in a traditional Odia household in the agrarian villages surrounding Khordha, Odisha. The rural setting is characterised by paddy fields, coconut groves and a village temple that serves as the social and religious hub. Her upbringing was infused with Odia customs such as the celebration of Raja, Durga Puja and the harvest festival Pana Sankranti, as well as the oral transmission of folk tales, dance, and music. The community’s emphasis on collective harmony and respect for elders shaped her worldview, while the region’s rich tradition of hand-loom weaving, temple art and ritual cuisine provided the canvas for her imaginative and artistic leanings.
payload.linguistic_background: Her mother tongue is Odia, which she uses fluently for all daily interactions, from household chores to community gatherings. Due to lifelong illiteracy, she cannot read or write in Odia script, but she possesses an excellent oral command, remembering verses, recipes and folk songs by heart. She has limited exposure to Hindi, mainly through occasional radio broadcasts and visits to the nearby market, but she does not speak it confidently. Her lack of formal schooling means she relies on visual cues and memory rather than written instruction.
payload.skills_and_expertise: She possesses practical household skills honed over decades of managing a large rural home. These include preparing a variety of traditional Odia cuisines, weaving simple hand-loom textiles, maintaining a kitchen garden, conducting family puja and religious ceremonies, storytelling through folk tales, and basic arithmetic for market transactions. Her artistic instinct also finds expression in rangoli design, bamboo craft, and improvisational singing during festivals.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Traditional Odia cooking", "Hand-loom weaving", "Kitchen gardening", "Conducting family puja and rituals", "Storytelling (folk tales)", "Basic arithmetic for market transactions", "Rangoli design", "Bamboo craft", "Improvisational singing during festivals", "Organising community celebrations"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: She delights in singing devotional Odissi bhajans, attending village fairs and Rath Yatra, creating colorful rangoli patterns, tending to her small garden of seasonal greens, listening to folk music on the radio, playing group games like Antakshari with neighbours, memorising and reciting classic Odia poetry, crafting simple jewellery from shells and beads, and engaging in storytelling sessions with her grandchildren.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Singing devotional Odissi bhajans", "Attending village fairs and Rath Yatra", "Creating colorful rangoli designs", "Tending a seasonal kitchen garden", "Listening to folk music on the radio", "Playing group games like Antakshari with neighbours", "Memorising and reciting classic Odia poetry", "Crafting simple jewellery from shells and beads", "Storytelling sessions with grandchildren"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: As a septuagenarian retired homemaker, she aspires to preserve and transmit her rich repository of Odia culinary, craft and folk knowledge to younger generations, especially her grandchildren. She aims to mentor local women in traditional cooking and weaving, volunteer in the village temple committee, and document oral stories so they are not lost. Maintaining good health, staying socially active, and contributing to community welfare are also central to her ambitions.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 79-year-old female from rural Odisha, Khordha district, who is widowed and retired, with an illiterate education level. She speaks Odia fluently but cannot read or write, and her lifestyle includes daily household chores and community activities. Her routine involves misplacing medication and forgetting appointments, and she has a history of hypertension.
