OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|...]]
Chief complaint: <short clinical line matching assigned domain>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Khordha District Hospital]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|KDH-OD-001]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Sushila Devi]] | DOB [[DOB|1945-07-22]] | Age: [[AGE|79]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Homemaker]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-0815-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Raghunath Mishra]]
Relative: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sanjay Kumar]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9438765432]]
Registrar EmpID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-4412]] | District: [[DISTRICT|Khordha]]
Chief complaint: Persistent cough for 3 weeks with low-grade fever and weight loss.

History of Present Illness:
The patient, a 79-year-old widowed female from a rural village in Khordha district, presents with a productive cough lasting approximately three weeks. The cough is worse in the mornings and is associated with scant, clear sputum. She reports low-grade evening fevers, which she measures at home as around 99.5°F (37.5°C). Over the past month, she has noted a gradual loss of appetite and an unintentional weight loss of about 3 kilograms. She has a known history of hypertension for the past 15 years, for which she takes medication irregularly. She denies any hemoptysis, chest pain, or shortness of breath.

Past Medical History:
- Hypertension (diagnosed 15 years ago)
- No known history of diabetes or cardiac disease
- No prior history of tuberculosis

Physical Examination:
- General: Thin, cachectic female, appears chronically ill.
- Vital Signs: BP 140/90 mmHg, Pulse 88/min, Respiratory Rate 18/min, Temperature 99.2°F (37.3°C), SpO2 96% on room air.
- Respiratory: Trachea central. Chest expansion is reduced bilaterally. Percussion reveals dullness in the right upper zone. Breath sounds are vesicular with diminished air entry in the right upper zone. No wheezes or crackles heard.
- Cardiovascular: S1 and S2 are audible, no murmurs.

Investigations:
- Sputum for AFB: 2 samples sent.
- Chest X-ray: Shows a right upper lobe infiltrate with cavitation.
- Blood: Hb 10.2 g/dL, TLC 8,200/cu.mm, Platelets 2.1 lakh/cu.mm.
- Blood Sugar (Fasting): 110 mg/dL.
- ECG: Normal sinus rhythm.

Diagnosis:
1. Pulmonary Tuberculosis, Right Upper Lobe
2. Hypertension

Treatment Plan:
The patient is started on the Revised National Tuberculosis Control Programme (RNTCP) Category I regimen.
- Isoniazid (H): 300 mg once daily
- Rifampicin (R): 450 mg once daily
- Pyrazinamide (Z): 1500 mg once daily
- Ethambutol (E): 750 mg once daily

She is advised to take the medication daily under observation for the first two months. A DOT provider, ASHA worker Sunita Patnaik, will be coordinating her treatment. She is also advised to continue her antihypertensive medication regularly. Follow-up sputum examination will be done after 2 months of treatment. Nutritional advice and counseling on medication adherence are provided.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

payload.generated_text:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|SCB Medical College Hospital]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-042]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Sushila Devi]] [[AGE|79]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Alok Mishra]]
Procedure / findings: The patient is a 79-year-old female from Khordha district, Odisha, presenting with a painful, irreducible right inguinal hernia of approximately 10-year duration. She reports increasing discomfort over the past month, with occasional episodes of nausea but no vomiting or bowel obstruction. On examination, a 7x5 cm firm, tender mass is palpable in the right inguinal region, extending to the scrotum. There is no evidence of overlying skin changes or crepitus. The patient is otherwise alert and oriented to person and place, though she occasionally forgets the date. The procedure was explained to the patient and her son, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sanjay Kumar]], who provided consent. The operation was performed under spinal anesthesia. A right inguinal herniotomy was performed via a standard incision. The sac was found to contain a loop of small intestine, which was reduced without any signs of ischemia. The sac was then ligated and excised. The floor of the inguinal canal was repaired using a Bassini's technique with non-absorbable sutures. The wound was closed in layers with absorbable sutures. The patient tolerated the procedure well and was shifted to the post-operative ward in [[BED_NUMBER|15]] on the general surgery ward. The patient's [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-0042]] and [[PATIENT_ID|PID-98765]] were used for registration. The patient will be observed for 24 hours post-operatively for any signs of recurrence or complications.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.

- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Radiology / Imaging Report for clinical domain Oncology / Complex Chronic Care.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Punjabi (code=pa, script=Gurmukhi). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Oncology / Complex Chronic Care — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Haryana, district=Sirsa.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Gurmukhi for Punjabi.

PLACEMENT: Include [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] as the reporting radiologist (e.g. Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]]) — do not leave the doctor name as untagged prose.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 39
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Haryana
- district: Sirsa
- first_language: Punjabi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 39-year-old female from Haryana, currently married, with a higher secondary education, who works as a homemaker. She speaks Punjabi and Hindi, and resides in an urban setting in Sirsa. Her lifestyle includes daily yoga, community service, and a budding interest in home catering.

Document format guide for Radiology / Imaging Report (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Imaging report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Findings: <radiology prose, untagged>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
Imaging report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sirsa Civil Hospital]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|Jaspreet Kaur]] [[AGE|39]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-0912-004]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0912-004]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vikram Singh]]
Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed to evaluate the known primary malignancy and assess treatment response. The liver shows multiple hypodense lesions, the largest measuring 3.2 cm in segment VIII, with peripheral rim enhancement and central necrosis, consistent with metastatic disease. No new hepatic lesions are identified compared to the prior study from three months ago. The pancreas is unremarkable. The spleen demonstrates a solitary 1.8 cm hypodense lesion with homogeneous enhancement, likely a hemangioma. The kidneys are normal in size and contour. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy is noted. The adrenal glands are unremarkable. The uterus and ovaries are within normal limits. No ascites or pleural effusion is seen. The bony structures are intact without acute fractures or lytic lesions. The findings indicate progression of hepatic metastatic burden.
Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vikram Singh]]
[[DISTRICT|Sirsa]]
[[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
[[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-HS-001]]
[[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete ER Triage & Nursing Notes for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Punjabi (code=pa, script=Gurmukhi). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Haryana, district=Sirsa.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Gurmukhi for Punjabi.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once in the arrival/RTA/ambulance line. Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 39
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Haryana
- district: Sirsa
- first_language: Punjabi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 39-year-old female currently married, retired homemaker from Haryana, with higher secondary education, fluent in Punjabi and Hindi. She resides in Sirsa, an urban district, and manages household duties while planning a catering business. Her lifestyle includes daily yoga, community service, and a diet rich in traditional Punjabi cuisine.

Document format guide for ER Triage & Nursing Notes (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|22]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|...]]
Arrived by ambulance / RTA vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|...]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Vitals / acuity: <short notes>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
payload.generated_text:
ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sirsa Civil Hospital]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0912-001]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Jaswinder Kaur]] [[AGE|39]] [[GENDER|Female]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|07]]
Arrived by ambulance vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|HR23AB5678]] (exactly once).
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|Gurpreet Singh]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vikram Chawla]]
Vitals / acuity: Patient is alert and oriented x3, complaining of severe right upper quadrant abdominal pain radiating to the back, associated with nausea and vomiting. Vitals: BP 140/90 mmHg, HR 105 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 101.2°F, SpO2 98% on room air. Pain score 8/10. Patient appears distressed.

Nursing Notes:
Patient arrived via ambulance at approximately 08:30 hours. Initial assessment reveals a 39-year-old female homemaker from Sirsa, Haryana, presenting with acute onset of sharp, constant right upper quadrant abdominal pain for the past 6 hours. Pain is described as 8/10 in severity, radiating to the right shoulder. Associated symptoms include two episodes of non-bilious vomiting and anorexia. No fever, chills, or jaundice reported. Past medical history is significant for hypertension, diagnosed 2 years ago. Patient is on antihypertensive medication but does not recall the name. Allergies: No known drug allergies. Family history is non-contributory. Social history: Non-smoker, occasional alcohol use.

On physical examination, the abdomen is soft with guarding and tenderness in the right upper quadrant. Murphy's sign is positive. Bowel sounds are hypoactive. No organomegaly palpable. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations are unremarkable. Neurological examination is within normal limits.

Initial investigations ordered: CBC, LFT, RFT, serum amylase, lipase, abdominal ultrasound, and ECG. IV access established with 18G cannula in the left forearm. Patient was started on IV fluids (Normal Saline 100 ml/hr) and analgesics (IV Diclofenac 75 mg). Blood samples collected for investigations. Patient is kept NPO pending further evaluation.

Plan: Admit to General Medicine ward for further management of acute cholecystitis versus biliary colic. Continue IV fluids, analgesics, and monitor vitals every 4 hours. Await ultrasound report. Consult surgical team if clinical condition deteriorates. Patient counseled about the condition and proposed management plan.

payload.professional_persona: A seasoned household manager and aspiring home‑caterer, Dhan Rani leverages their meticulous budgeting skills and renowned north‑Indian kitchen expertise; they orchestrate family celebrations while laying the groundwork for a local catering business.
payload.linguistic_persona: A multilingual communicator, Dhan Rani speaks Punjabi fluently in Gurmukhi, is equally comfortable in Hindi for daily affairs, and they handle functional English for online tasks and smartphone use.
payload.sports_persona: A casual fitness enthusiast, Dhan Rani practices yoga daily, follows Indian Premier League cricket matches, roots for the Haryana women's kabaddi team, and they participate in community health walks during festive seasons, though they rarely engage in competitive sports.
payload.arts_persona: An avid lover of music and drama, Dhan Rani enjoys listening to bhangra beats by Gurdas Maan, heartfelt bhajans by Anup Jalota, watches Hindi TV serials starring Kirti Kulhari, and they occasionally stitch Phulkari embroidery while humming classic Bollywood melodies.
payload.travel_persona: A thoughtful traveler, Dhan Rani plans weekend trips to nearby pilgrim towns like Kurukshetra and Amritsar for cultural immersion, dreams of a family holiday in the Swiss Alps, and they balance travel with school fees for their children, preferring affordable train journeys over flights.
payload.culinary_persona: A passionate home chef, Dhan Rani expertly prepares sarson da saag, makki di roti, gajar ka halwa, and butter chicken, they relish the aroma of mustard seeds and ghee, prefer fresh farm‑grown vegetables, and they enjoy dining out at dhabas for their smoky tandoori kebabs.
payload.persona: A diligent planner with a sweet tooth, Dhan Rani balances household duties, community service, and a budding catering dream while sprinkling everyday life with bhangra rhythm, and they occasionally indulge in impulsive gadget splurges.
payload.cultural_background: Dhan Rani hails from a Punjabi Hindu family that has been settled in Sirsa, Haryana for generations. While her household follows the vibrant customs of Punjab such as celebrating Lohri, Baisakhi, and cooking rich Punjabi fare, she also embraces the local Haryanvi ethos of straightforwardness and community solidarity. Growing up in an urban setting, she has been exposed to both traditional values and modern conveniences, which cultivates a balanced outlook that respects heritage while staying open to new ideas.
payload.linguistic_background: Dhan Rani's mother tongue is Punjabi, and she is comfortable speaking, reading, and writing it in the Gurmukhi script. She is equally fluent in Hindi, which she uses for everyday interactions, official matters, and reading local newspapers. Her schooling up to higher secondary equipped her with functional English, enabling her to read signage, use a smartphone, and follow basic online information. Overall, she is multilingual, with strong command over Punjabi and Hindi, and limited but functional English.
payload.skills_and_expertise: Dhan Rani excels in managing a large household, juggling budgeting, grocery shopping, and meal planning with precision. She is an adept cook of Punjabi cuisine, known for preparing dishes like sarson da saag, makki di roti, and rich desserts for festivals. Her organized nature makes her a reliable event planner for family celebrations, religious ceremonies, and community gatherings. She possesses basic computer and smartphone skills, handling budgeting spreadsheets, online bill payments, and staying connected with relatives via WhatsApp. Additionally, she is skilled in simple sewing, phulkari embroidery, and first-aid basics, and often volunteers at the local temple committee.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household management", "Budgeting", "Punjabi cooking", "Event planning", "Basic computer literacy", "Smartphone usage", "Phulkari embroidery", "Sewing", "First-aid basics", "Community volunteering"]
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Dhan Rani enjoys spending time in the kitchen, experimenting with traditional Punjabi recipes and perfecting festive sweets. She loves listening to Punjabi folk tunes and bhajans, occasionally joining bhangra sessions during family gatherings. In her leisure, she gardens a small vegetable patch, practices yoga for wellness, and indulges in knitting and phulkari embroidery. She also follows popular Hindi TV serials, reads devotional books, and participates in community events and temple festivals.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Cooking Punjabi dishes", "Listening to Punjabi folk music and bhajans", "Bhangra dancing", "Gardening", "Yoga", "Knitting", "Phulkari embroidery", "Watching Hindi TV serials", "Reading devotional books", "Participating in community and temple festivals"]
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Looking ahead, Dhan Rani aspires to turn her passion for Punjabi cooking into a modest home-based catering venture, offering authentic dishes for local events and festivals. She also wishes to enhance her English proficiency to communicate more confidently online and explore digital platforms for sharing recipes. Additionally, she aims to support her children's higher education, take on a leadership role in the local temple committee, and continue volunteering for women-empowerment workshops. Her long-term ambition is to blend her domestic expertise with entrepreneurial spirit, creating a sustainable source of income while preserving cultural traditions.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 39-year-old female currently married and retired from a homemaker role, residing in urban Haryana. She is fluent in Punjabi and Hindi, with a higher secondary education. Clinically, she has a sedentary lifestyle with occasional gadget use and a diet rich in traditional Punjabi fare.

- zone: Urban

- district: Sirsa
- first_language: Punjabi

--- chat ---

payload.generated_text:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Jaswinder Kaur]] [[AGE|39]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|jaswinder.kaur@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Civil Hospital Sirsa]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Namaste doctor saheb, I am [[PATIENT_NAME|Jaswinder Kaur]] from Sirsa. I am 39 years old and I am currently 18 weeks pregnant with my second child. I have been having some pain in my lower abdomen for the past two days, it's a dull ache but sometimes it becomes sharp. I also feel a bit of pressure down there. I have not noticed any bleeding or fluid leakage. My first delivery was a normal one.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]: Hello Jaswinder ji, thank you for reaching out. I understand you are concerned about the abdominal pain at 18 weeks. First, I need to confirm a few things. Are you having any contractions, like your uterus tightening and then relaxing? And how is your baby moving?
Patient: No contractions, doctor. The pain is not rhythmic. The baby is moving normally, I can feel him kicking. The pain is more when I walk or stand for a long time. It feels better when I lie down on my left side.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]: That is good to hear that the baby is moving well. The pain you are describing sounds like round ligament pain, which is very common in pregnancy as your uterus grows. It happens when the ligaments that support the uterus stretch. Since it gets better with rest on your left side, that is a positive sign. I want you to be very careful. Please avoid any heavy lifting or strenuous activity. Try to rest as much as you can. You can also try applying a warm compress to the area.
Patient: Okay doctor, I will try to rest more. What about my diet? I am eating my regular Punjabi food. Should I change anything?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]: Your diet sounds fine. Just ensure you are eating a balanced meal with plenty of fiber to prevent constipation, which can sometimes cause discomfort. Drink lots of water throughout the day. If the pain becomes severe, if you start bleeding, if you have a fever, or if you feel the baby's movements decrease significantly, you must go to the [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Civil Hospital Sirsa]] emergency department immediately. We will schedule a follow-up call in two days to check on you.
Patient: Thank you so much, doctor. I will be careful and call you if anything changes.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]: You are most welcome, Jaswinder ji. Take care and have a healthy pregnancy.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):
  [[AADHAAR_NUMBER|157815659387]]

  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:

  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:

GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.

payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete ASHA Worker Note for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Sanskrit (code=sa, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Uttar Pradesh, district=Kanpur Nagar.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Devanagari for Sanskrit.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 19
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Matric/Secondary
- zone: Urban
- state: Uttar Pradesh
- district: Kanpur Nagar
- first_language: Sanskrit
- clinical_persona_summary: A 19‑year‑old male from Kanpur Nagar, Uttar Pradesh, with a matriculation education, currently not employed. He is unmarried and lives in an urban zone, speaking Sanskrit as his first language with functional English proficiency. His lifestyle includes occasional anxiety, yoga, and a preference for street food, with a cultural background that blends Islamic traditions and classical Indian arts.

Document format guide for ASHA Worker Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ASHA note — Village [[VILLAGE|...]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Beneficiary [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|26]] / [[GENDER|Female]]
ASHA: [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|...]] | Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Visit findings: <2-4 short sentences>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- ASHA_WORKER_NAME — ASHA Worker Name e.g. [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
- VILLAGE — Village e.g. [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- BPL_RATION_CARD — BPL / Ration Card Number e.g. [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
- VOTER_ID — Voter ID e.g. [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
- RELIGION — Religion e.g. [[RELIGION|Hindu]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.

payload.professional_persona: Dehuri Ghasi, a market stall operator in Mayurbhanj, combines meticulous inventory management and persuasive price negotiation with a curiosity for integrating traditional crafts into modern retail, and their disciplined, achievement‑driven mindset ensures every display meets high standards despite the stress of fluctuating sales.
payload.linguistic_persona: Dehuri Ghasi, a 25‑year‑old who speaks Santali fluently in Ol Chiki script, has functional Odia for daily transactions, picks up bits of Hindi from market chatter, and uses basic English learned from TV and tourists to navigate occasional digital payments.
payload.sports_persona: Dehuri Ghasi, who enjoys watching IPL cricket and follows the Odisha state team, participates in early morning jogs through village groves, occasionally joins a local kabaddi club for weekend matches, and prefers walking as his primary fitness routine.
payload.arts_persona: Dehuri Ghasi, a passionate admirer of Sohrai wall paintings, often sketches forest motifs inspired by traditional patterns, listens avidly to folk singer Jitu Murmu, and cites A. R. Rahman's film scores as a bridge between his traditional roots and contemporary music.
payload.travel_persona: Dehuri Ghasi, who has visited Puri's Jagannath Temple and the historic Konark Sun Temple, dreams of exploring the monsoon‑clad valleys of Mawsynram, and plans a future trip to Tokyo to learn about digital marketplaces that could empower his artisan cooperative.
payload.culinary_persona: Dehuri Ghasi, who relishes the tangy flavor of pakhala bhata with mango pickle, enjoys seasonal bamboo shoot curry, savors dalma made with pumpkin and mustard greens, and often snacks on roasted mahua flowers for their earthy aroma.
payload.persona: Energetic yet anxious Dehuri Ghasi, a 25‑year‑old market stall owner, blends meticulous organization with a love for folk art, but often lets worry over sales keep them up past midnight.
payload.cultural_background: Dehuri Ghasi comes from the tribal heartland of Mayurbhanj district in Odisha. He belongs to the Santal community, an Adivasi group renowned for its oral epics, vibrant folk music, dance forms like Chhau, and distinctive visual arts such as Sohrai and Kajari wall paintings. The agrarian calendar, together with festivals like Sohrai (post-harvest), Baha (spring) and Karam (tree-worship), shapes his daily rhythm. Living in a close-knit rural village, he has imbibed values of collective harmony, respect for nature, and a natural curiosity about how traditional crafts can intersect with new ideas.
payload.linguistic_background: His mother tongue is Santali, and he reads and writes in the Ol Chiki script taught in his tribal school. After completing middle school, he acquired functional literacy in Odia, which he employs for reading market signs, filling simple forms, and handling cash transactions. He also picks up bits of Hindi and basic English from customers, television, and occasional visitors, but he does not have formal training or advanced proficiency in either language.
payload.skills_and_expertise: As a stall and market salesperson, Dehuri excels in inventory management, product presentation, cash handling, and price negotiation. His strong interpersonal communication lets him build trust with regular customers, while his disciplined approach ensures meticulous record-keeping and efficient workflow. He also possesses basic knowledge of traditional Santali crafts, enabling him to guide buyers seeking authentic tribal artefacts. His time-management skills and attention to detail help mitigate his worries about market fluctuations.
payload.skills_and_expertise_list: ['Inventory Management', 'Product Presentation', 'Cash Handling', 'Price Negotiation', 'Interpersonal Communication', 'Meticulous Record Keeping', 'Basic Knowledge of Santali Crafts', 'Time Management', 'Attention to Detail']
payload.hobbies_and_interests: Dehuri enjoys exploring folk art, especially Sohrai paintings, and frequently sketches motifs inspired by the surrounding forest and farmland. He is an avid listener of traditional Santali music and often joins community dances during festivals. In his spare time, he reads simple books on entrepreneurship and self-improvement, maintaining a personal journal to track his goals and reflections. He occasionally takes photographs of rural scenes with his smartphone for future creative projects. For relaxation, he walks through nearby groves, practices quiet meditation under sacred trees, and occasionally experiments with cooking traditional tribal dishes.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Sketching folk motifs', 'Appreciating Sohrai paintings', 'Listening to Santali music', 'Participating in community dance', 'Reading entrepreneurship books', 'Maintaining a personal journal', 'Smartphone photography of rural scenes', 'Nature walks and meditation', 'Cooking traditional tribal dishes']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Dehuri aims to expand his modest stall into a well-organized retail outlet that showcases authentic Santali crafts alongside essential daily goods. He plans to enroll in short-term courses on small-business management, digital payment systems, and e-commerce marketing to modernise his operations. In the next five years, he envisions establishing a cooperative that aggregates products from other tribal artisans in Mayurbhanj, creating a brand recognized across Odisha and eventually in metropolitan markets. By doing so, he hopes to secure sustainable income for his family, reduce the stress of unpredictable sales, and preserve his cultural heritage.
payload.persona_clinical_summary: A 25-year-old male from Mayurbhanj, Odisha, works as a market stall salesperson, with middle school education and a rural occupation. He is never married, speaks Santali as his first language, and is functionally literate in Odia. Clinically, he presents with anxiety related to sales, which may impact sleep, and his tribal background from the Santal community may influence his health beliefs and communication style. His lifestyle includes early morning jogs and a preference for walking.

- state: Odisha

Clinically, he presents with anxiety related to sales, which may impact sleep, and his tribal background from the Santal community may influence his health beliefs and communication style. - AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]

payload.generated_text:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-01]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Mayurbhanj District Hospital]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. S. K. Patra]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Bikash Murmu]], [[AGE|25]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|Mayurbhanj]]
Reason: Patient presents with persistent cough, low-grade fever, and weight loss, requiring further evaluation for suspected pulmonary tuberculosis and assessment of comorbid non-communicable diseases.

Dear Colleague,

We are referring [[PATIENT_NAME|Bikash Murmu]] for specialized evaluation. The patient is a 25-year-old male from the Santal community, residing in a rural area of Mayurbhanj district. He works as a market stall salesperson and reports significant anxiety related to his work, which he states affects his sleep patterns.

On examination, the patient is thin, with a body mass index of 17.8 kg/m². He has a chronic productive cough for the past three months, occasional evening fevers, and has lost approximately 5 kg in weight over the last two months. His vitals are stable. A chest X-ray was performed at our facility, showing infiltrates in the right upper lobe. Sputum samples have been sent for GeneXpert testing. Given his clinical presentation and radiological findings, we are highly suspicious of pulmonary tuberculosis.

Additionally, the patient reports a history of palpitations and occasional dizziness, which may be related to stress and anxiety. We would like the receiving physician to assess for any underlying cardiac or metabolic conditions.

For your reference, the patient's details are as follows:
Medical Record Number: [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
Phone Number: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9437891234]]
Relative Contact: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Smt. Chita Murmu]] (Mother)

We would appreciate your expert opinion and management plan for this patient. Please find attached the relevant investigation reports.

Sincerely,
Dr. S. K. Patra
[[HOSPITAL_NAME|Mayurbhanj District Hospital]]
[[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-OD-023]]
[[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-001]]

[[PASSPORT_NUMBER|P8765432]]
[[DRIVING_LICENCE|OD14 20200012345]]

payload.professional_persona: Dehuri Ghasi, a market stall operator in Mayurbhanj, combines meticulous inventory management and persuasive price negotiation with a curiosity for integrating traditional crafts into modern retail, and their disciplined, achievement‑driven mindset ensures every display meets high standards despite the stress of fluctuating sales.
payload.linguistic_persona: Dehuri Ghasi, a 25‑year‑old who speaks Santali fluently in Ol Chiki script, has functional Odia for daily transactions, picks up bits of Hindi from market chatter, and uses basic English learned from TV and tourists to navigate occasional digital payments.
payload.sports_persona: Dehuri Ghasi, who enjoys watching IPL cricket and follows the Odisha state team, participates in early morning jogs through village groves, occasionally joins a local kabaddi club for weekend matches, and prefers walking as his primary fitness routine.
payload.arts_persona: Dehuri Ghasi, a passionate admirer of Sohrai wall paintings, often sketches forest motifs inspired by traditional patterns, listens avidly to folk singer Jitu Murmu, and cites A. R. Rahman's film scores as a bridge between his traditional roots and contemporary music.
payload.travel_persona: Dehuri Ghasi, who has visited Puri's Jagannath Temple and the historic Konark Sun Temple, dreams of exploring the monsoon‑clad valleys of Mawsynram, and plans a future trip to Tokyo to learn about digital marketplaces that could empower his artisan cooperative.
payload.culinary_persona: Dehuri Ghasi, who relishes the tangy flavor of pakhala bhata with mango pickle, enjoys seasonal bamboo shoot curry, savors dalma made with pumpkin and mustard greens, and often snacks on roasted mahua flowers for their earthy aroma.
payload.persona: Energetic yet anxious Dehuri Ghasi, a 25‑year‑old market stall owner, blends meticulous organization with a love for folk art, but often lets worry over sales keep them up past midnight.
payload.cultural_background: Dehuri Ghasi comes from the tribal heartland of Mayurbhanj district in Odisha. He belongs to the Santal community, an Adivasi group renowned for its oral epics, vibrant folk music, dance forms like Chhau, and distinctive visual arts such as Sohrai and Kajari wall paintings. The agrarian calendar, together with festivals like Sohrai (post-harvest), Baha (spring) and Karam (tree-worship), shapes his daily rhythm. Living in a close-knit rural village, he has imbibed values of collective harmony, respect for nature, and a natural curiosity about how traditional crafts can intersect with new ideas.
payload.linguistic_background: His mother tongue is Santali, and he reads and writes in the Ol Chiki script taught in his tribal school. After completing middle school, he acquired functional literacy in Odia, which he employs for reading market signs, filling simple forms, and handling cash transactions. He also picks up bits of Hindi and basic English from customers, television, and occasional visitors, but he does not have formal training or advanced proficiency in either language.
payload.skills_and_expertise: As a stall and market salesperson, Dehuri excels in inventory management, product presentation, cash handling, and price negotiation. His strong interpersonal communication lets him build trust with regular customers, while his disciplined approach ensures meticulous record-keeping and efficient workflow. He also possesses basic knowledge of traditional Santali crafts, enabling him to guide buyers seeking authentic tribal artefacts. His time-management skills and attention to detail help mitigate his worries about market fluctuations.

payload.hobbies_and_interests: Dehuri enjoys exploring folk art, especially Sohrai paintings, and frequently sketches motifs inspired by the surrounding forest and farmland. He is an avid listener of traditional Santali music and often joins community dances during festivals. In his spare time, he reads simple books on entrepreneurship and self-improvement, maintaining a personal journal to track his goals and reflections. He occasionally takes photographs of rural scenes with his smartphone for future creative projects. For relaxation, he walks through nearby groves, practices quiet meditation under sacred trees, and occasionally experiments with cooking traditional tribal dishes.
payload.hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Sketching folk motifs', 'Appreciating Sohrai paintings', 'Listening to Santali music', 'Participating in community dance', 'Reading entrepreneurship books', 'Maintaining a personal journal', 'Smartphone photography of rural scenes', 'Nature walks and meditation', 'Cooking traditional tribal dishes']
payload.career_goals_and_ambitions: Dehuri aims to expand his modest stall into a well-organized retail outlet that showcases authentic Santali crafts alongside essential daily goods. He plans to enroll in short-term courses on small-business management, digital payment systems, and e-commerce marketing to modernise his operations. In the next five years, he envisions establishing a cooperative that aggregates products from other tribal artisans in Mayurbhanj, creating a brand recognized across Odisha and eventually in metropolitan markets. By doing so, he hopes to secure sustainable income for his family, reduce the stress of unpredictable sales, and preserve his cultural heritage.

payload.system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

  [[INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER|POL-PB-2024-7781]]

  [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

  [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]

  [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]
  [[VILLAGE|Shivpur]]
  [[VOTER_ID|ABC1234567]]
  [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]

Latin/digits OK inside these entity values even for Indic documents: AADHAAR_NUMBER, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, DRIVING_LICENCE, VOTER_ID, BPL_RATION_CARD, TAX_ID, PHONE_NUMBER, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PIN_CODE, IP_ADDRESS, URL, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, IFSC_CODE, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, PIN, EMPLOYEE_ID, STUDENT_ID, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, ABHA_ID, ABHA_ADDRESS, MRN, PATIENT_ID, HOSPITAL_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, ENCOUNTER_ID, APPOINTMENT_ID, REFERRAL_ID, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, WARD_NUMBER
Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete Radiology / Imaging Report for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Santali (code=sat, script=Ol Chiki). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.

ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Mayurbhanj.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Ol Chiki for Santali.

PLACEMENT: Include [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] as the reporting radiologist (e.g. Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]]) — do not leave the doctor name as untagged prose.

- sex / gender: Male
- age: 25
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Stall and Market Salespersons, Other
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Rural
- state: Odisha
- district: Mayurbhanj
- first_language: Santali

Imaging report — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] | [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|45]] [[GENDER|Female]]
MRN [[MRN|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]] | Reported by Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Findings: <radiology prose, untagged>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]

- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- ABHA_ID — ABHA ID e.g. [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.

- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:

BAD → instruction_drift:

GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.

  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):

GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.
payload.user_prompt:
Write one complete ER Triage & Nursing Notes for clinical domain Surgical.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Santali (code=sat, script=Ol Chiki). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
CRITICAL: TYPE names are NEVER translated or localized — always exact ASCII allow-list (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). Forbidden: [[রোগীর_নাম|…]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]].
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Surgical — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.
GEO ANCHOR: any DISTRICT/VILLAGE/HOSPITAL_NAME must be plausible for persona state=Odisha, district=Mayurbhanj.
NOTE: translation stage (not this draft) will put the body into Ol Chiki for Santali.

PLACEMENT (vehicle): Put VEHICLE_REGISTRATION exactly once in the arrival/RTA/ambulance line. Do not dump or repeat it.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 25
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Stall and Market Salespersons, Other
- education_level: Middle
- zone: Rural
- state: Odisha
- district: Mayurbhanj
- first_language: Santali
- clinical_persona_summary: A 25-year-old male from Mayurbhanj, Odisha, works as a market stall salesperson, with middle education and a rural background. He is never married, speaks Santali as his first language, and is functionally literate in Odia. Clinically, he presents with anxiety related to sales, which may impact sleep.

Document format guide for ER Triage & Nursing Notes (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|22]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|...]]
Arrived by ambulance / RTA vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|PB10AB1234]] (exactly once).
Relative [[RELATIVE_NAME|...]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|...]] Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Vitals / acuity: <short notes>

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ENCOUNTER_ID — Encounter / Visit ID e.g. [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-55601]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION — Vehicle Registration Number e.g. [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|UP78AB1234]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ER triage — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Mayurbhanj District Hospital]] Encounter [[ENCOUNTER_ID|ENC-2024-0815-001]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Soren Murmu]] [[AGE|25]] [[GENDER|Male]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|ER]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|05]]
Arrived by ambulance vehicle [[VEHICLE_REGISTRATION|OD10A1234567]]
